proposals.title: Freshening and Wind-Driven Destratification in the Subpolar North Atlantic: A Mechanistic Test of Phytoplankton Community and Trophic Consequences
proposals.motivation: The canonical view is that ocean warming and ice melt enhance surface stratification globally, suppressing nutrient supply and favoring small phytoplankton. However, observations in the subpolar North Atlantic gyre (SPG) show a complex interplay of strong freshening and intense wind forcing. This research addresses the critical, unresolved question of whether these combined forces can instead reduce seasonal stratification, with profound implications for primary production, carbon export, and ecosystem structure in a key climate-regulating region.
proposals.hypothesis: Amplified high-latitude precipitation/runoff, when coupled with strong wind forcing, drives a net decrease in seasonal stratification in the SPG by reducing salinity-driven density gradients and enhancing wind-driven mixing, shifting the phytoplankton community from picoplankton-dominated to one supporting larger diatoms.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite (SeaWIFS/MODIS ocean color, SMOS/SMAP salinity, QuikSCAT/ASCAT winds) and in-situ (Argo floats, OOI, CPR survey) data from 2003-2025 to detect coherent freshening and mixing signals. High-resolution regional MITgcm simulations with biogeochemical (DARWIN) modules will be forced with CMIP6-endorsed scenarios to attribute stratification changes to the proposed mechanism. Plankton time-series will be analyzed alongside trait-based models (e.g., Diat-HadOCC) to quantify shifts in community size structure and export potential, using metrics like the mixed layer depth anomaly and the phytoplankton size index.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, falsifiable framework for understanding counter-trend stratification dynamics in subpolar gyres. It will directly link physical drivers to shifts in phytoplankton functional types and carbon export pathways, offering a critical test for next-generation Earth system models that currently struggle to represent high-latitude biogeochemical feedbacks accurately.
proposals.novelty: The proposal challenges the uniform stratification paradigm by testing a specific, adversarial hypothesis of net destratification. It uniquely integrates satellite salinity, high-resolution physical-biogeochemical modeling, and trait-based plankton ecology to mechanistically connect hydrographic change to ecosystem restructuring.

proposals.title: Regional Destratification in a Warming Arctic: Identifying Oceanographic Forcings that Counteract Thermal Layering
proposals.motivation: Climate models project that ocean warming will increase upper-ocean stratification, reducing nutrient supply to the euphotic zone and fundamentally altering phytoplankton communities. However, this uniform assumption overlooks complex regional dynamics. Drawing from atmospheric boundary layer studies where localized forcings can decrease stability, this project investigates whether analogous oceanographic processes could mitigate or reverse stratification trends in key regions, with critical implications for primary production and carbon export predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that regional anomalies in wind stress, freshwater input, and lateral advection can generate localized decreases in water column stratification in the Arctic Ocean, counteracting the basin-scale trend of increased thermal layering under climate change.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution (≤1 km) regional configuration of the MITgcm for the Pacific Arctic sector (Bering/Chukchi Seas), forced by CMIP6 end-of-century scenarios. The model will be optimized with biogeochemical modules (e.g., BLING) to track nutrient fluxes. We will analyze model outputs using Lagrangian particle tracking and a stratification number (analogous to the atmospheric Richardson number) to identify destratification events. These will be validated against historical (2003-2025) satellite-derived sea surface salinity, ice-melt data, and in-situ hydrographic profiles from programs like NASA's ICESCAPE and BOEM/NOAA cruises.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new framework for assessing ocean stratification that incorporates destabilizing forcings, moving beyond purely temperature-driven predictions. It will deliver specific, testable predictions for regions where phytoplankton bloom phenology and community structure (e.g., diatoms vs. picoplankton) may deviate from current models due to sustained nutrient supply, thereby refining projections of Arctic marine ecosystem response to climate change.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in directly applying the conceptual framework of atmospheric boundary layer stability exceptions to ocean stratification, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously explored in this context. It challenges the canonical view by systematically searching for and quantifying dynamic oceanic 'destratification' hotspots.

proposals.title: Phytoplankton as Ecosystem Engineers: Quantifying Biophysical Feedbacks on Mixed Layer Stratification in the Changing Arctic Ocean
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is experiencing rapid physical and ecological change, with earlier ice melt and more frequent under-ice phytoplankton blooms. Current models treat mixed layer depth (MLD) as a purely physical driver of phytoplankton dynamics, but this unidirectional view may miss critical feedbacks where biology modifies physics. Understanding these biophysical couplings is essential for accurate projections of Arctic primary production and carbon export in a warming climate.
proposals.hypothesis: Phytoplankton communities can actively modify ocean stratification and mixed layer depth through two primary mechanisms: (1) altering the vertical profile of solar heating via species-specific light attenuation and (2) generating microscale turbulence via the sinking of aggregates and active swimming, creating a biologically-mediated feedback loop on their physical environment.
proposals.method: We will develop a 1-D coupled biophysical model, building on established frameworks like the General Ocean Turbulence Model (GOTM) coupled to a nutrient-phytoplankton-zooplankton-detritus (NPZD) model. Novel terms will be explicitly added: a bio-optical heating module where phytoplankton absorption and scattering alter the penetrative shortwave radiation profile, and a biogenic turbulence source term parameterized from aggregate sinking dynamics and zooplankton motility. The model will be initialized and validated using pre-2026 observational data from the Chukchi Sea and pan-Arctic bloom analyses, with sensitivity experiments to isolate biological forcing impacts on MLD.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new mechanistic framework for understanding phytoplankton as active modifiers of their physical habitat. It will deliver quantitative estimates of the significance of biological feedbacks on Arctic Ocean stratification, challenging the paradigm of purely top-down physical control. The resulting model improvements will enhance predictions of bloom timing, magnitude, and biogeochemical cycles.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the classical causality in oceanography by treating phytoplankton as analogous to ecosystem engineers in geomorphology, directly forcing physical models. While light attenuation effects are studied, the explicit coupling of biogenic turbulence generation to stratification dynamics in a predictive model is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Circumpolar Test of a Physical-Biological Link: A Standardized Observational Network to Determine the Universality of Mixed Layer Depth Control on Phytoplankton Community Structure in the Southern Ocean
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean is a critical region for global carbon cycling and climate, yet its primary productivity is governed by complex, regionally variable physical-biological interactions. Studies like those in the Ross Sea suggest mixed layer depth (MLD) is a key determinant of phytoplankton community composition and bloom dynamics, but it is unknown if this relationship is a local feature or a circumpolar principle. Resolving this is essential for accurately modeling Southern Ocean biogeochemistry and predicting its response to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed inverse relationship between mixed layer depth and the dominance of larger, bloom-forming phytoplankton taxa (e.g., diatoms) is a universal, circumpolar feature of the Southern Ocean, driven by light availability and stratification dynamics, rather than a phenomenon specific to the Ross Sea sector.
proposals.method: We will deploy a standardized observational network across the major Southern Ocean sectors (Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, and Ross Sea) using a combination of instrumented moorings, biogeochemical Argo floats, and coordinated ship-based process studies. All platforms will carry identical sensor suites (e.g., CTD, fluorometers, optical backscatter, nitrate sensors) and employ harmonized protocols for discrete water sampling (for HPLC pigment analysis, microscopy, and POC/N). Data will be integrated and analyzed using a unified processing pipeline, with MLD calculated via a density threshold method (Δσ=0.03 kg m⁻³) and community structure derived from diagnostic pigment indices (e.g., CHEMTAX) and size-fractionated chlorophyll.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first directly comparable, circumpolar dataset of synchronized physical and biological variables, enabling a robust test of a fundamental ecological principle. It will clarify the primary controls on Southern Ocean productivity, directly improving the parameterization of phytoplankton functional types in regional and global biogeochemical models (e.g., MITgcm, ROMS).
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the individual measurements, but in the epidemiological cohort-study approach: implementing strict, multi-platform standardization across the entire Southern Ocean to disentangle universal drivers from regional contingencies, a strategy not yet applied at this scale in polar oceanography.

proposals.title: Resilience of Arctic Phytoplankton Interaction Networks to Regionally Divergent Stratification Forcing
proposals.motivation: Arctic Ocean stratification is not changing monotonically; regional freshening and increased storminess can lead to decreased or more heterogeneous stratification, particularly in shelf seas. These complex physical changes are poorly represented in ecosystem models that assume simple, increasing stratification, leading to uncertain predictions of phytoplankton community composition and carbon export. Understanding how key competitive networks, like the foundational diatom-Phaeocystis system, respond to these nuanced drivers is critical for projecting Arctic biogeochemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: The stability and modularity of the diatom-Phaeocystis interaction network are more sensitive to the spatial heterogeneity and regional sign (increase/decrease) of stratification changes than to the pan-Arctic mean trend, with network properties dictating where community shifts will deviate from broad-scale expectations.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite-derived stratification indices (2003-2025) and CMIP6 model projections to map regions of projected stratification increase, decrease, and increased heterogeneity. A trait-based ecological network model, parameterized with pre-2026 Arctic field data on light, nutrients, and grazing, will simulate the diatom-Phaeocystis competition. Network stability (eigenvalue analysis), modularity, and keystone species indices will be calculated under each regional stratification scenario using algorithms from ecological network analysis (ENA).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Arctic map of phytoplankton network vulnerability to non-monotonic stratification, directly linking physical drivers to ecological network properties. It will advance predictive capacity by identifying specific regions (e.g., the Beaufort Sea shelf vs. the Eurasian Basin) where community responses are likely to be counter-intuitive, thereby refining projections of future Arctic primary production and biogeochemical cycles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying ecological network resilience metrics to assess phytoplankton community responses to regionally divergent—not just uniformly increasing—physical forcing, a critical step beyond the prevailing paradigm in Arctic ecosystem modeling.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Historical Arctic Ocean Stratification and Carbon Export via Paleo-Data Assimilation of Sedimentary Diatom and Phaeocystis Assemblages
proposals.motivation: Understanding past changes in Arctic Ocean stratification is critical for contextualizing the rapid, anthropogenically-driven changes observed in recent decades. Current paleoceanographic reconstructions of mixed layer depth (MLD) rely on transfer functions that lack explicit dynamical constraints, limiting their process-based interpretation and temporal resolution. A method that dynamically links phytoplankton ecology to physical drivers would provide a more robust, mechanistic history of upper ocean structure and its impact on carbon cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble-based paleo-data assimilation framework, using sedimentary diatom and Phaeocystis assemblages as observations, can inversely solve for a physically and ecologically consistent time series of historical mixed layer depth and carbon export in the Arctic Ocean.
proposals.method: We will employ an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) to solve the inverse problem. An ensemble of 1D biogeochemical models (e.g., configured from frameworks like FVCOM-ICM or MITgcm) will simulate phytoplankton community succession in response to variable MLD, light, and nutrients (nitrate, iron). The forward model output (simulated fossil assemblages) will be statistically compared to down-core microfossil data from key Arctic regions (e.g., Chukchi Sea). Running the EnKF inversely will iteratively adjust the ensemble's prior MLD states to optimally fit the sediment observations, yielding a posterior reconstruction of MLD and associated carbon export fluxes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dynamically-constrained, high-resolution reconstruction of Arctic Ocean stratification and export production, directly linking phytoplankton ecology to physics. It will move the field beyond static transfer functions, providing a new tool to test hypotheses about the drivers of past ecosystem shifts and to benchmark climate model simulations of the Arctic marine system.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying advanced data assimilation techniques from numerical weather prediction to the paleoceanographic inverse problem, creating a dynamic, process-based framework instead of a static statistical mapping. It explicitly uses the ecological competition between diatoms and Phaeocystis, known to be sensitive to stratification, as a direct constraint on past physical conditions.

proposals.title: Resolving Sub-Grid Heterogeneity: A High-Resolution Biogeochemical Model of Arctic Shelf and Pelagic Productivity Using Satellite and In Situ Data Fusion
proposals.motivation: Current Arctic biogeochemical models often treat shelf and pelagic regions as internally uniform, despite pre-2026 evidence of intense spatial heterogeneity in phytoplankton blooms driven by variable light, nutrient, and iron availability. This coarse representation limits the accuracy of carbon export and ecosystem projections under rapid climate change. A higher-resolution understanding is critical for predicting future Arctic primary production and its role in the global carbon cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling fine-scale spatial heterogeneity within Arctic shelf and pelagic domains, driven by physical and chemical fronts, will significantly improve the simulation of primary production patterns and vertical carbon export compared to models using uniform regional productivity assumptions.
proposals.method: We will develop a 1km-resolution regional ocean biogeochemical model for the Chukchi and Beaufort Seas, building on frameworks like the Regional Ocean Modeling System (ROMS) coupled to a nutrient-phytoplankton-zooplankton-detritus (NPZD) model with iron dynamics. The model will be initialized and validated using a fusion of pre-2026 satellite ocean color data (MODIS, VIIRS), NASA's ICESat-2 data for under-ice light, and in situ biogeochemical data from programs like the NASA EXPORTS and NSF Arctic Observing Network. Key algorithms will include a spectral light model and a trait-based phytoplankton module to resolve community shifts across gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, high-resolution model capable of simulating sub-regional productivity hotspots and carbon export, directly addressing gaps in pan-Arctic analyses. It will provide a mechanistic framework to quantify how frontal processes and ice melt dynamics control the spatial heterogeneity of blooms, refining future projections of Arctic marine ecosystem response to climate change.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of emerging high-resolution satellite and in situ data streams pre-2026 to parameterize and validate a biogeochemical model at a scale that captures the sub-mesoscale heterogeneity within broad Arctic shelf and pelagic provinces, moving beyond the uniform productivity paradigm.

proposals.title: Assimilating Under-Ice and MIZ Observations into a Coupled Biogeochemical Model to Constrain Pan-Arctic Primary Production Estimates
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of Arctic Ocean primary production is critical for understanding carbon cycling and ecosystem responses to rapid climate change. However, existing model-based estimates are highly uncertain, particularly for under-ice and marginal ice zone (MIZ) blooms, due to sparse and heterogeneous observations. A systematic framework is needed to merge diverse in situ, satellite, and autonomous platform data to produce physically consistent, high-resolution gridded fields of phytoplankton biomass and growth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that assimilating a diverse suite of ocean color, chlorophyll fluorescence, and nutrient observations into a regional ice-ocean-biogeochemical model using an ensemble-based data assimilation scheme will significantly reduce uncertainty in estimated pan-Arctic primary production, especially in the poorly observed seasonal ice zone.
proposals.method: We will employ the Regional Ocean Modeling System (ROMS) coupled to a biogeochemical module (e.g., Fennel or CoSiNE) configured for the Arctic. An Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) will be implemented to assimilate satellite-derived ocean color (when available), in situ chlorophyll profiles from Argo floats and ship campaigns, and nitrate data from biogeochemical Argo floats. The assimilation will specifically target periods and regions identified for under-ice and MIZ blooms in prior analyses. The system will produce daily, three-dimensional, dynamically consistent analyses of phytoplankton biomass and growth rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Arctic, data-assimilated reanalysis of primary production, providing a benchmark dataset for ecological and climate studies. It will quantitatively constrain the magnitude and spatial-temporal patterns of under-ice primary production, a key but uncertain component of the Arctic carbon budget. The methodology will demonstrate a pathway to integrate traditional and emerging autonomous observations into operational biogeochemical forecasting.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is mature in physical oceanography, its application to constrain Arctic primary production by jointly assimilating optical and chemical tracers for under-ice ecosystems is novel. This approach moves beyond simple model-data comparison to statistically optimal synthesis, directly addressing spatial and temporal gaps in Arctic observations.

proposals.title: Quantifying Key Trophic Pathways and Carbon Flow Vulnerabilities in the Changing Arctic Marine Ecosystem Using Economic Input-Output Analysis
proposals.motivation: The Arctic marine ecosystem is undergoing rapid physical and biogeochemical changes, including sea ice loss and shifts in phytoplankton bloom phenology, which fundamentally alter carbon flow from primary production to higher trophic levels and export. Understanding which trophic transfer pathways are most critical for carbon sequestration versus ecosystem support is essential for predicting the Arctic's future role in the global carbon cycle and food web stability. Pre-2026, network analysis of carbon flow has been limited, lacking a formal framework to quantify the systemic importance of specific species or functional groups.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying economic input-output analysis (EIOA) to an Arctic carbon flow network will reveal that a small subset of trophic pathways, potentially involving under-ice phytoplankton blooms and key microzooplankton grazers, are disproportionately responsible for system-wide carbon transfer and are highly sensitive to climate-driven perturbations.
proposals.method: We will construct a mass-balanced carbon flow network for the Chukchi Sea shelf region using pre-2026 data from Arrigo's prior work and literature, defining compartments (nodes) for phytoplankton (including ice-algal), bacteria, microzooplankton, mesozooplankton, and export. The Leontief inverse matrix will be calculated to trace direct and indirect carbon flows. Key analyses will include calculating trophic multipliers (total carbon flow triggered by a unit of primary production) and backward linkage indices to identify compartments with high systemic influence. Network perturbation analysis will simulate changes in key compartments (e.g., diatoms, copepods) to assess cascading effects on carbon export and food web efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, system-level identification of the most critical and vulnerable carbon transfer pathways in the Arctic shelf seas. It will move beyond descriptive food webs to a predictive framework, directly linking climate-induced shifts in primary production (bloom timing, species composition) to consequences for carbon sequestration and higher trophic levels like fish and marine mammals. The results will offer a novel metric for evaluating ecosystem resilience.
proposals.novelty: While EIOA is a cornerstone of economics for analyzing supply chains, its application to quantify carbon 'supply chains' in marine ecosystems is novel. This approach provides a rigorous, matrix-based method to calculate the total carbon footprint of different phytoplankton bloom types and grazing pathways, which has not been done in the context of a rapidly changing Arctic.

proposals.title: Quantifying Melt Pond Evolution and Light Transmission in the Marginal Ice Zone Using High-Resolution Satellite Imagery and Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: The rapid decline of Arctic sea ice is fundamentally altering the light environment for under-ice phytoplankton blooms, a key research theme of Kevin Robert Arrigo. Melt ponds dramatically reduce surface albedo and modulate light penetration, but their spatiotemporal heterogeneity is poorly quantified in large-scale models. This creates major uncertainty in estimates of photosynthetically available radiation (PAR) for primary production and in ice-albedo feedback projections within climate models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning-based segmentation of high-resolution satellite imagery will reveal a non-linear relationship between melt pond fraction and transmitted PAR, and that this relationship varies significantly between the dynamic marginal ice zone (MIZ) and the consolidated pack ice interior.
proposals.method: We will apply a U-Net convolutional neural network architecture, pre-trained on land cover and fine-tuned with manually labeled melt pond masks from Sentinel-2 (10m) and PlanetScope (3m) imagery, to generate pan-Arctic melt pond maps for spring/summer 2013-2025. The segmentation will classify pixels as snow, bare ice, melt pond, and open water. Outputs will be coupled with downwelling irradiance data and a radiative transfer model to compute transmitted PAR. Analysis will focus on contrasting regions like the Chukchi Sea MIZ (a known bloom region from Arrigo's prior work) with the central Arctic.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, validated dataset of melt pond fraction and derived light transmission at unprecedented spatial resolution for the Arctic. It will directly improve parameterizations of light availability for biogeochemical models of under-ice phytoplankton production and provide critical validation data for climate model representations of the ice-albedo feedback.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have used spectral indices for melt pond detection, the application of state-of-the-art computer vision (U-Net) to very high-resolution commercial satellite imagery (PlanetScope) for pan-Arctic light transmission estimates is novel. This approach directly bridges remote sensing with the specific biophysical light requirements for phytoplankton, a core focus of Arrigo's research program.

proposals.title: Sub-seasonal phenology of Arctic phytoplankton production: A scale-aware analysis of bloom initiation, duration, and termination in a changing ice-ocean system
proposals.motivation: Arctic marine primary production is increasing due to sea ice loss, but annual integrated totals mask critical sub-seasonal dynamics. The timing, duration, and termination of the productive season control carbon export efficiency and food web match-mismatch, with consequences for biogeochemistry and higher trophic levels. Understanding these phenological shifts is urgent as the Arctic transitions to a more Atlantic-influenced, stratified state with potential for increased nitrification and altered iron cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sub-seasonal phenology of Arctic phytoplankton production is controlled by scale-dependent interactions between ice retreat timing, upper ocean stratification, and nutrient (nitrate, iron) depletion rates, leading to regionally divergent trends in bloom duration and carbon export potential not predictable from annual production alone.
proposals.method: We will analyze a 20-year (2003-2023) satellite-derived chlorophyll-a and PAR record (MODIS/VIIRS), coupled with biogeochemical Argo float profiles and ocean reanalysis data (e.g., ECCO, GLORYS). A multi-scale analytical framework will be employed: 1) A novel algorithm to detect bloom initiation, peak, and termination dates at each pixel, defining phenological phases; 2) A wavelet coherence analysis to relate phenological metrics to physical drivers (ice concentration, mixed layer depth, frontal activity) across temporal scales from weekly to seasonal; 3) A regional clustering analysis to identify distinct phenological regimes and their recent trends, informed by known iron and nitrate limitations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first pan-Arctic, scale-explicit quantification of phytoplankton phenological phases, directly linking their variability to physical and biogeochemical forcing. It will advance beyond bulk annual metrics to predict how changing bloom duration and termination mechanisms modulate vertical carbon export, addressing a key uncertainty in climate-biogeochemistry models. The analysis will inform future observational campaigns targeting critical transition periods.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped bloom frequency or annual totals, this study uniquely decomposes the productive season into distinct phenological phases and explicitly analyzes their physical controls across temporal scales, integrating pre-2026 knowledge of under-ice blooms, frontal subduction, and nutrient constraints to explain regional disparities in phenological change.

proposals.title: Unmasking Marginal Ice Zone Productivity: Quantifying Algorithmic Biases in Satellite Ocean Color Retrievals for the Pan-Arctic
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived estimates suggest Marginal Ice Zone (MIZ) phytoplankton productivity is only marginally higher than under-ice regions, challenging ecological models that predict intense, light-driven blooms at the ice edge. This discrepancy is critical because the MIZ is a rapidly expanding biome due to Arctic sea ice loss, and accurate productivity estimates are essential for understanding carbon export and food web dynamics. Pre-2026 studies, including Arrigo's pan-Arctic bloom analyses, highlight the need to reconcile satellite observations with in-situ data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard satellite ocean color algorithms systematically underestimate MIZ phytoplankton biomass and primary production due to errors in atmospheric correction and chlorophyll-a retrieval in high-backscatter, optically complex waters characteristic of melting ice.
proposals.method: We will conduct a targeted field campaign in the Chukchi Sea MIZ, collecting high-resolution vertical profiles of spectral irradiance, particle absorption/backscatter, and phytoplankton pigments. These in-situ inherent optical properties (IOPs) will be used to regionally tune and validate alternative atmospheric correction (e.g., POLYMER) and bio-optical algorithms (e.g., IOP-based versus OCx empirical). We will then apply these refined algorithms to a 20-year MODIS/VIIRS satellite record, comparing corrected MIZ productivity estimates against legacy NASA Standard products and concurrent biogeochemical Argo float data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitatively robust, pan-Arctic correction to satellite-derived MIZ primary production, directly addressing a key uncertainty in Arctic carbon budgets. It will deliver an improved, publicly available algorithm suite for high-latitude ocean color processing and clarify the true magnitude and phenology of MIZ blooms, informing projections of future Arctic ecosystem response.
proposals.novelty: While algorithm limitations are known, this study uniquely focuses on the specific scattering/reflectance anomalies of the dynamic MIZ, moving beyond open-ocean or under-ice optimizations to directly test if the perceived 'slight' productivity increase is a methodological artifact rather than a biological reality.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Primary Drivers of Pan-Arctic Phytoplankton Bloom Phenology: A Comparative Analysis of Sea Ice, Mixed Layer Depth, and Nutrient Supply
proposals.motivation: The dramatic reduction in Arctic sea ice cover is widely assumed to be the principal driver of changes in phytoplankton bloom timing and magnitude, potentially increasing primary production. However, this paradigm may oversimplify a complex system where physical stratification (mixed layer depth) and nutrient availability, influenced by circulation and freshwater input, could exert equal or greater control. Resolving the relative importance of these drivers is critical for accurate biogeochemical forecasting in a rapidly changing Arctic Ocean.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interannual and regional variability in mixed layer depth and pre-bloom nutrient inventories are more fundamental determinants of phytoplankton bloom initiation and magnitude than sea ice concentration alone, with ice cover acting as a secondary modulator of light availability.
proposals.method: We will synthesize satellite-derived sea ice concentration, chlorophyll-a, and sea surface height (for inferring fronts) with in situ data from biogeochemical Argo floats, moorings, and ship campaigns (2003-2024) across key Arctic regions (e.g., Chukchi, Barents, Beaufort Seas). A machine learning framework (Random Forest or Gradient Boosting regression) will be used to perform a comparative importance analysis, quantifying the relative contribution of ice cover, mixed layer depth (from float profiles), and nitrate/silicate concentrations to predicting bloom start date and peak biomass. Statistical models will control for regional differences and temporal autocorrelation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, pan-Arctic ranking of environmental drivers of phytoplankton phenology, moving beyond correlative studies. It will directly test the prevailing sea ice-centric paradigm and identify regions where changes in vertical stratification or nutrient supply may outweigh the effects of ice loss, thereby refining projections of future Arctic carbon cycling and ecosystem function.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents blooms under ice or in marginal ice zones, a systematic, data-driven comparison explicitly challenging sea ice as the primary driver across the entire Arctic is lacking. The novel integration of float-derived subsurface nutrient and mixing data with satellite time series for a machine learning-based driver attribution represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Arctic Primary Production Hotspots: The Potential for Earlier Stratification to Drive Pelagic Blooms
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean's traditional pelagic-shelf dichotomy identifies productive continental shelves and nutrient-poor central basins. Rapid sea ice loss and earlier melt onset are fundamentally altering the physical drivers of phytoplankton growth, particularly the timing and extent of stratification. This project investigates whether these changes could invert the established paradigm, making pelagic regions unexpectedly productive and altering carbon export pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Earlier sea ice retreat and meltwater input in the central Arctic Ocean will induce stratification sufficiently early in the growing season to trap nutrients in the euphotic zone, thereby transforming pelagic regions into transient but significant phytoplankton bloom hotspots.
proposals.method: We will synthesize satellite-derived sea ice concentration, sea surface temperature, and ocean color (MODIS-Aqua, VIIRS) from 2003-2025 for the pan-Arctic. Using a 1-D mixed-layer model forced by reanalysis data (ERA5), we will simulate the timing of stratification onset and its correlation with bloom initiation. This analysis will be coupled with an assessment of nutrient fields from historical biogeochemical Argo floats and shipboard datasets to evaluate pre-bloom nutrient inventories in pelagic vs. shelf regions. Statistical models (generalized additive models) will test the relative importance of stratification timing versus nutrient availability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, observationally-constrained framework to predict shifts in Arctic primary production geography under continued warming. It will directly inform models of carbon export and ecosystem function by challenging the static shelf-pelagic productivity divide. The findings will be critical for projecting future biogeochemical states of the central Arctic.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (including Arrigo's) has documented under-ice and MIZ blooms, this proposal explicitly tests the inversion of a core ecological paradigm—the unproductive pelagic gyre—by focusing on the phenological shift in stratification, a previously underexplored trigger for pelagic productivity.

proposals.title: The Emergent Role of Sea Surface Temperature in Controlling Pan-Arctic Phytoplankton Bloom Phenology and Magnitude Under Rapid Warming
proposals.motivation: The primary controls on Arctic phytoplankton productivity have historically been light availability (via ice cover) and nutrient supply, with sea surface temperature (SST) considered a secondary, minor factor. However, the Arctic is warming at more than twice the global average rate, leading to dramatic reductions in sea ice extent and thickness. This shift fundamentally alters the physical and optical environment, potentially elevating the importance of SST for phytoplankton growth rates and bloom dynamics in newly ice-free waters, a hypothesis not yet rigorously tested at the pan-Arctic scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that as the Arctic Ocean continues to warm and sea ice diminishes, SST will transition from a minor, background factor to a primary, statistically significant control on the timing, peak magnitude, and spatial distribution of phytoplankton blooms across the Arctic Ocean.
proposals.method: We will leverage the 2003-2025 satellite record (MODIS-Aqua, VIIRS) to create pan-Arctic maps of bloom phenology metrics (bloom onset, peak timing, magnitude) and climatological SST. Using a multivariate statistical framework, including generalized additive models (GAMs) and causal network inference algorithms (e.g., PCMCI), we will quantify the time-varying strength of SST as a predictor of bloom metrics relative to static predictors (bathymetry) and dynamic covariates (ice retreat date, surface winds, photosynthetically available radiation). The analysis will explicitly test for temporal non-stationarity by evaluating model coefficients and explained variance in rolling 5-year windows across the satellite era.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, pan-Arctic assessment of the evolving hierarchy of environmental controls on phytoplankton production. It will directly inform next-generation biogeochemical models by identifying if and where temperature-dependent growth functions require re-parameterization. The findings will clarify the future trajectory of Arctic primary production and associated carbon export, a critical uncertainty in climate projections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Arrigo and others has established light and nutrients as dominant controls, this proposal explicitly tests for a temporal *shift* in control mechanisms—a non-stationary response—rather than a static correlation. It moves beyond documenting changes in bloom frequency to mechanistically attribute those changes to a changing climate variable (SST) whose influence is presumed to be increasing.

proposals.title: Sub-mesoscale iron and light modulation of phytoplankton production at Arctic shelf-break fronts: A high-resolution modeling study
proposals.motivation: Basin-scale models of Arctic primary production fail to capture the intense, localized blooms observed at shelf-break fronts and eddies, which are critical for carbon export and food web dynamics. Pre-2026 studies, including Arrigo's work on under-ice blooms and frontal subduction, highlight the importance of fine-scale physical processes in delivering limiting nutrients (like iron) and modulating light availability, but the integrated mechanistic understanding at the sub-mesoscale (1-10 km) remains a key gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sub-mesoscale shelf-break fronts and eddies enhance local primary production by creating vertical fluxes of iron-rich Pacific-origin waters into the photic zone, and that the net effect on phytoplankton growth is a non-linear function of concurrent physical modulation of mixed layer depth and light exposure.
proposals.method: We will employ the Regional Ocean Modeling System (ROMS) coupled to a biogeochemical module (e.g., Fennel-type) configured for the Chukchi Sea shelf break at ultra-high resolution (~500 m horizontal grid). The model will be forced with realistic 2015-2020 atmospheric reanalysis and boundary conditions. We will specifically track iron and nitrate tracers with distinct Pacific/Atlantic signatures. Lagrangian particle tracking and Eulerian diagnostics will quantify vertical velocities, frontal slumping, and their correlation with simulated phytoplankton production and export flux.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based explanation for the high spatial variability in Arctic shelf-break productivity, quantifying the relative importance of iron supply versus light dynamics at the sub-mesoscale. It will directly inform the interpretation of satellite bloom analyses and sparse ship-based observations by revealing the underlying physical drivers, improving parameterizations for coarser climate models.
proposals.novelty: While frontal impacts are known, this study uniquely integrates the high-resolution physical dynamics of iron delivery from specific water masses with phytoplankton light response, moving beyond bulk nitrate limitation to address the coupled iron-light limitation paradigm at the scale of individual eddies and fronts where observations are sparse.

proposals.title: Mapping Benthic Nitrogen Loss in the Pan-Arctic: A Remote Sensing Approach to Identify Sedimentary Denitrification Hotspots from Ocean Color and Environmental Drivers
proposals.motivation: Sedimentary denitrification is a critical but poorly constrained sink in the marine nitrogen cycle, particularly in the rapidly changing Arctic Ocean where warming and altered primary production may shift nutrient budgets. Current estimates rely on sparse, point-based sediment core measurements, creating a major gap in our understanding of regional and pan-Arctic nitrogen fluxes. A scalable, satellite-based method is urgently needed to identify hotspots and quantify the spatial and temporal variability of this process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sedimentary denitrification hotspots in the Arctic shelf seas can be inferred from satellite-derived proxies for organic carbon export flux and benthic environmental conditions, which are themselves detectable through ocean color, sea surface temperature, and ice cover data.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-step algorithm using pre-2026 satellite data (MODIS, VIIRS, Sentinel-3). First, we will use existing regional algorithms for chlorophyll-a and particulate organic carbon (POC) to estimate export flux via the Carbon-based Productivity Model (CbPM). Second, we will integrate these fluxes with gridded data on bottom depth, sediment type (from bathymetry proxies), and seasonal hypoxia risk (inferred from stratification indices and surface POC). A machine learning model (Gradient Boosting Regression) will be trained on the existing sparse dataset of sedimentary denitrification rates to identify the key remote-sensing predictors and generate pan-Arctic maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Arctic, climatological map of potential sedimentary denitrification hotspots, directly linking surface processes to benthic nutrient cycling. It will provide a novel observational constraint for biogeochemical models and a framework for monitoring how climate-driven changes in Arctic primary production and ice cover may alter nitrogen availability.
proposals.novelty: While satellite ocean color is routinely used to study phytoplankton, applying it to quantitatively map a benthic microbial process like denitrification is a novel, non-obvious synthesis. The approach uniquely combines carbon export models with benthic habitat proxies in a data-driven framework specifically tailored for the complex Arctic shelves.

proposals.title: A Pan-Arctic Assessment of Sea Ice Retreat and Net Primary Production Trends: Extending the Satellite Record to 2025
proposals.motivation: Satellite-based records of Arctic Ocean net primary production (NPP) have been critical for quantifying the biological response to rapid sea ice loss, but many foundational analyses, including the seminal work by Arrigo et al. (2008), are limited to data through 2009. The subsequent decade (2010-2025) has witnessed unprecedented climatic extremes, including record-low sea ice extents and pronounced Atlantification and Pacification of Arctic shelves. Extending the NPP time series is essential to determine if earlier trends have accelerated, stabilized, or shifted regionally, directly informing predictions of future carbon cycling and ecosystem function in a seasonally ice-free Arctic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strong positive trend in pan-Arctic NPP observed from 1998-2009 has continued through 2025, but that the spatial pattern of change has shifted, with growth rates slowing on some interior shelves while accelerating in the marginal ice zones of the Central Arctic due to increased light availability and nutrient supply mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will extend the analysis of Arrigo et al. (2008, 2011) by applying the same vertically generalized production model (VGPM) to merged ocean color data from MODIS-Aqua (2002-2022), VIIRS (2012-2025), and OLCI-Sentinel 3A/B (2016-2025), ensuring cross-sensor consistency. Daily sea ice concentration from SSM/I, SSMIS, and AMSR2 will be used to classify pixels as open water, under-ice, or marginal ice zone. NPP will be calculated on a 6.25 km grid, with annual integrals and trends analyzed for the full pan-Arctic domain and key bioregions (e.g., Chukchi, Barents, Central Arctic). Statistical trend analysis will employ the Mann-Kendall test and Theil-Sen slope estimator.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the most comprehensive, consistent, and up-to-date (1998-2025) satellite record of Arctic NPP, directly quantifying the integrated biological impact of 25 years of sea ice decline. It will identify emerging 'hotspots' and 'coldspots' of production change, providing an essential observational benchmark for next-generation biogeochemical models and policy-relevant assessments of Arctic marine productivity.
proposals.novelty: While recent studies have analyzed under-ice blooms or regional production, this proposal uniquely provides a continuous, pan-Arctic NPP record spanning the entire MODIS era and bridging into the VIIRS/OLCI era, explicitly linking the post-2009 acceleration of physical change to its biological consequences using a consistent, proven methodology.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Projections of Under-Ice Phytoplankton Blooms in a Warming Arctic Using Dynamical Downscaling
proposals.motivation: Coarse-resolution global climate models (GCMs) poorly resolve the marginal ice zone and sub-ice light fields critical for Arctic phytoplankton blooms, as evidenced by recent observational analyses. This limitation creates high uncertainty in projections of future Arctic Net Primary Production (NPP) and associated biogeochemical feedbacks under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: Dynamical downscaling of CMIP6 future scenarios will reveal that GCMs systematically underestimate the frequency and magnitude of future under-ice phytoplankton blooms by failing to capture fine-scale sea ice dynamics and associated light availability.
proposals.method: We will use the Regional Ocean Modeling System (ROMS) coupled to a sea ice model and a biogeochemical module (e.g., Fennel-type NPZD with iron limitation) to downscale CMIP6 SSP5-8.5 scenario outputs for the Pacific Arctic sector (Bering/Chukchi Seas). The downscaling will employ a 5-km grid, using spectral nudging for atmospheric boundary conditions. Key outputs will include daily fields of ice thickness, photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) transmittance, and NPP, analyzed to quantify bloom phenology.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, process-oriented projections of under-ice bloom dynamics for the 21st century, directly informing predictions of carbon export and ecosystem shifts. It will quantify the bias in coarse-model NPP projections and provide improved regional scenarios for resource management.
proposals.novelty: While downscaling has been applied for physical oceanography, its targeted application to resolve the critical light-ice-phytoplankton nexus for future Arctic NPP projections is novel, integrating Dr. Arrigo's expertise in bloom detection with advanced regional climate modeling.

proposals.title: Quantifying Under-Ice Phytoplankton Biomass and Net Primary Production in a Changing Arctic: Integrating Acoustic Backscatter with Satellite and Biogeochemical Observations
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived Net Primary Production (NPP) estimates are critically limited in the Arctic Ocean, particularly for under-ice and marginal ice zone blooms, due to ice cover and complex optical properties. Pre-2026 studies, including Arrigo's pan-Arctic bloom analysis, highlight the importance of these hidden blooms for carbon export and ecosystem dynamics, but validation of satellite NPP models with in-situ biomass data remains a major gap, especially in the context of rapid sea-ice decline and altered nutrient regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibrated acoustic backscatter from shipboard and moored instruments can provide high-resolution, depth-resolved estimates of phytoplankton carbon biomass, and that integrating this data will significantly improve the accuracy of satellite-based NPP models in ice-influenced Arctic waters by providing essential validation and constraining vertical biomass distribution.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new acoustic data (38 and 120 kHz) from the Chukchi and Beaufort Seas, collected concurrently with biogeochemical measurements (chlorophyll, POC, nutrients, iron). Acoustic backscatter will be converted to carbon biomass using region-specific empirical relationships derived from net tows and LISST data. This biomass time series will be used to validate and recalibrate satellite NPP algorithms (e.g., Vertically Generalized Production Model) by providing critical in-situ carbon data, with a focus on correcting for sub-surface biomass maxima and under-ice production missed by ocean color sensors. A key technical step will be the application of neural network-based fusion models to merge acoustic biomass profiles with satellite-derived surface properties and physical reanalysis data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, acoustically-constrained NPP product for the Arctic, directly addressing uncertainties in carbon flux estimates. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how physical processes (e.g., frontal subduction) captured in acoustic data influence vertical carbon export. The validated methodology will establish a framework for using autonomous acoustic platforms (e.g., gliders) for long-term NPP monitoring in ice-covered seas.
proposals.novelty: While acoustics are used for zooplankton and fish, their systematic application to validate and correct satellite NPP estimates for phytoplankton, specifically targeting the under-ice realm where optical methods fail, is a novel integration. This moves beyond correlative studies to a quantitative, mechanistic fusion of acoustic remote sensing with satellite biogeochemistry.

proposals.title: Sub-mesoscale Ice-Ocean-Biology Coupling: Quantifying the Role of Ice-Edge Eddies in Modulating Phytoplankton Productivity in a Retreating Arctic Marginal Ice Zone
proposals.motivation: Arctic sea ice loss is dramatically increasing the extent and duration of the Marginal Ice Zone (MIZ), a region of high but heterogeneous biological productivity. While pan-Arctic trends in under-ice blooms are emerging, the physical mechanisms controlling the patchiness and magnitude of net primary production (NPP) at the ice edge remain poorly resolved. Sub-mesoscale eddies (1-10 km) are ubiquitous in the MIZ and are hypothesized to be critical, yet unresolved, modulators of light and nutrient availability for phytoplankton, creating local hotspots that regional models fail to capture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cyclonic ice-edge eddies enhance local NPP responses to regional ice loss by sustaining phytoplankton blooms through a combination of frontal nutrient supply, mixed-layer modulation, and increased light exposure from eddy-induced ice divergence.
proposals.method: We will synthesize high-resolution satellite data (Sentinel-1 SAR for sea ice motion and eddy detection, MODIS/VIIRS for ocean color) with in situ biogeochemical data from autonomous platforms (gliders, floats) and historical ship campaigns in the Chukchi and Beaufort Seas. A coupled physical-biological analysis will be performed using a Lagrangian approach to track eddy evolution and quantify associated NPP. Key metrics will include eddy-induced upwelling velocity (derived from sea surface height anomalies), subsurface nitrate and iron inventories (from glider profiles), and phytoplankton growth rates estimated from a bio-optical model incorporating light history and nutrient stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, process-based linkage between observed ice-edge eddy activity and localized NPP anomalies in the Arctic MIZ. It will deliver parameterizations of eddy-driven nutrient supply and bloom persistence for inclusion in regional biogeochemical models. The findings will refine predictions of carbon export potential and ecosystem response to ongoing ice retreat.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond correlative pan-Arctic bloom analyses to mechanistically diagnose the sub-mesoscale physical drivers, explicitly testing the role of eddies in modulating the ice-loss productivity response—a scale not addressed by Arrigo's prior MIZ bloom climatologies or carbon export studies.

proposals.title: Early-Season Net Primary Production as a Predictor of Pan-Arctic Sea Ice Melt Dynamics: A Biophysical Feedback Assessment
proposals.motivation: Arctic sea ice decline is accelerating, yet predictive models of summer ice state remain uncertain, particularly regarding biological feedbacks. Pre-2026 research by Arrigo and others has established that under-ice and marginal ice zone phytoplankton blooms are widespread and occur earlier in the season, altering ocean heat content and albedo. Quantifying the causal link between this early-season biological activity and subsequent physical ice melt is critical for improving seasonal forecasts and understanding climate feedbacks in a rapidly changing Arctic.
proposals.hypothesis: Early-season (April-June) net primary production (NPP) in the Arctic Ocean, by influencing upper-ocean absorption of solar radiation and stratification, is a significant and quantifiable predictor of the rate and spatial pattern of sea ice retreat later in the summer (July-September).
proposals.method: We will synthesize satellite-derived NPP (using standard ocean color algorithms like OC-CCI and regionally tuned bio-optical models) and sea ice concentration/extent data (NSIDC) from 2003-2024 across the pan-Arctic domain. A multivariate lagged correlation analysis will first identify spatiotemporal relationships. We will then employ a Random Forest regression model, trained on early-season NPP metrics, sea ice initial conditions, and atmospheric reanalysis data (ERA5), to predict end-of-summer minimum ice extent and regional melt patterns. Model performance will be validated against withheld years and compared to physics-only baseline predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Arctic, observationally based statistical model quantifying the predictive skill of biological activity for sea ice state. It will directly test the strength of the "biological pump on albedo" feedback mechanism. The results will provide crucial empirical constraints for next-generation climate models that are beginning to incorporate interactive biogeochemistry into their sea ice projections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented under-ice blooms and their biogeochemical impact, this study uniquely frames early-season NPP as a predictive *driver* of physical ice melt dynamics, moving beyond correlation to build a predictive framework. It integrates Arrigo's expertise in high-latitude algal blooms and carbon export with a novel machine-learning approach to ice prediction.

proposals.title: Decoupling Chlorophyll from Phytoplankton Biomass in a Warming Arctic: Assessing the Proxy's Validity Under Shifting Light and Nutrient Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) is the primary metric for assessing pan-Arctic phytoplankton biomass and primary production, underpinning climate models and ecosystem forecasts. However, rapid sea-ice loss and altered stratification are fundamentally changing the light and nutrient regimes experienced by phytoplankton, which can decouple cellular Chl-a from carbon biomass. This threatens the foundational assumption that satellite Chl-a reliably tracks biomass, potentially leading to significant errors in estimates of carbon fixation and export in this critical region.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Chl-a to carbon (Chl:C) ratio in Arctic phytoplankton communities exhibits systematic, predictable shifts under the novel light-nutrient regimes created by sea-ice retreat, rendering satellite Chl-a an increasingly biased proxy for particulate organic carbon (POC) biomass in certain oceanographic domains.
proposals.method: We will synthesize in situ data from Arctic cruises (2003-2025) measuring concurrent Chl-a, POC, nutrients, and light profiles. Using a machine learning framework (Gradient Boosting Regression), we will model the environmental drivers (surface irradiance, mixed layer depth, nitrate, iron) of the Chl:C ratio. This empirical model will be applied to satellite and reanalysis data to create pan-Arctic maps of predicted Chl:C bias. We will then validate these maps and assess bloom detection accuracy using dedicated field campaigns in the Chukchi and Beaufort Seas, targeting under-ice, marginal ice zone, and open-water conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Arctic, seasonally resolved correction algorithm to convert satellite Chl-a to phytoplankton carbon biomass, directly improving carbon export and primary production estimates. It will deliver a critical uncertainty metric for satellite-based biogeochemical models and identify specific regions and bloom types where the Chl-a proxy is most likely to fail.
proposals.novelty: While the physiological plasticity of Chl:C is known, this study moves beyond lab cultures to build a predictive, pan-Arctic empirical model of this decoupling using real-world environmental gradients, explicitly linking large-scale satellite remote sensing to the mechanistic underpinnings of phytoplankton photophysiology in a changing icescape.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Light-Limitation Paradigm: Assessing the Future Role of Nutrient Co-Limitation in a Warming, Ice-Free Arctic Ocean
proposals.motivation: Arctic Ocean primary production is projected to increase due to sea ice retreat and extended light availability. The prevailing paradigm, supported by pre-2026 observations, is that light is the dominant limiting factor, with nutrient limitation considered secondary. However, accelerated warming, enhanced stratification, and altered nutrient supply pathways may shift this balance, making it critical to test this assumption for accurate future projections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for specific Arctic shelf and basin regions, future increases in phytoplankton production will be co-limited by bioavailable nitrogen and iron, not solely by light, due to intensified stratification and changes in nitrification and land-ocean nutrient fluxes.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite-derived sea ice, chlorophyll, and light data (2003-2025) with a regional biogeochemical model (e.g., MITgcm-REcoM2) configured for the Pacific Arctic. The model will be forced with CMIP6 future climate scenarios (SSP5-8.5) to 2050. We will conduct factorial model experiments to isolate the effects of light, nitrate, and iron on net primary production, using a nutrient limitation index algorithm to quantify relative limitation strengths. Validation will leverage historical field data on vertical carbon export and under-ice blooms from the Chukchi and Beaufort Seas.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, spatially explicit assessment of the shifting hierarchy of limiting factors in the Arctic, moving beyond qualitative assumptions. It will directly inform climate models by identifying regions where nutrient dynamics, not light, will constrain future productivity and carbon export potential.
proposals.novelty: While light limitation is well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates observed trends in under-ice blooms and frontal subduction with future projections to test the *persistence* of the light-limitation paradigm under rapid environmental change, explicitly linking physical dynamics to nutrient (N, Fe) bioavailability.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Drivers of Mesopelagic Carbon Attenuation: Resolving the Link Between Surface Production Regimes and Benthic Carbon Supply in the Changing Arctic Ocean
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is experiencing rapid climate-driven shifts in primary production, including increased open-water duration and under-ice blooms. While surface productivity may rise, the efficiency of the biological carbon pump in transferring this carbon to depth is poorly constrained and may be modulated by mesopelagic food webs. Quantifying this transfer efficiency is critical for predicting the future role of the Arctic in the global carbon cycle and deep-sea carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficiency of carbon transfer through the mesopelagic zone is not a simple function of surface export flux but is primarily regulated by the composition and grazing activity of the mesopelagic zooplankton and microbial community, which vary predictably with surface phytoplankton assemblage and physical forcing.
proposals.method: We will deploy a multi-tool observational array at two contrasting sites in the Chukchi Sea: a high-production marginal ice zone and a seasonally ice-free shelf region. The array will include surface-to-seafloor moored sediment traps (McLane PARFLUX), laser optical particle counters (LISST-Deep) on profiling moorings, and an in-situ imaging system (UVP6) to capture particle flux, size spectrum, and type. Concurrent high-resolution water column profiling (CTD-rosette) will collect samples for environmental DNA (18S/16S rRNA metabarcoding) and traditional microscopy to characterize the consumer community from microbes to zooplankton.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a mechanistic, process-based understanding of how specific planktonic consumers attenuate carbon flux, moving beyond empirical relationships. It will directly inform and validate key parameters in regional biogeochemical models (e.g., SINMOD) used to project future carbon sequestration. The study will provide a foundational dataset linking changing surface regimes to benthic carbon supply in a climatically sensitive region.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies, including our own, have quantified export magnitude, this project uniquely integrates high-resolution particle dynamics with concurrent, depth-resolved genomic and visual characterization of the entire remineralizing community to explicitly test the mechanistic drivers of attenuation.

proposals.title: Scaling of Pelagic-Benthic Coupling in a Changing Arctic: A Metabolic and Stoichiometric Framework
proposals.motivation: Climate-driven changes in the Arctic, including sea ice loss and altered nutrient regimes, are increasing phytoplankton productivity and shifting bloom phenology. The fate of this carbon—whether it fuels the pelagic food web or is exported to the seafloor—critically impacts benthic ecosystems and the ocean's biological carbon pump. However, a quantitative, predictive framework linking changes in surface production to the strength of pelagic-benthic coupling is lacking, hindering forecasts of Arctic ecosystem function and carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: The strength of pelagic-benthic coupling in Arctic shelf seas scales predictably with surface primary production rate and the stoichiometric quality (C:N:P:Chl) of sinking particles, such that increased production under climate change will alter both the magnitude and metabolic efficiency of carbon transfer to the benthos.
proposals.method: We will synthesize data from historical Arctic cruises (e.g., SHELFZ, ICESCAPE) and satellite-derived productivity to establish baseline scaling relationships. Concurrently, we will conduct new field campaigns in the Chukchi and Barents Seas, deploying sediment traps and conducting benthic incubation chambers to measure vertical carbon export (POC, PON), particle lability (via enzymatic assays), and benthic community respiration. The core analytical framework will adapt the Metabolic Theory of Ecology and ecological stoichiometry, using Bayesian hierarchical models to parameterize coupling strength as a function of production rate, particle composition, and sinking velocity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first mechanistic, scaling-based model for pelagic-benthic coupling in the Arctic, moving beyond simple correlation. It will directly quantify how climate-driven shifts in bloom magnitude and phytoplankton community structure alter seafloor carbon supply, providing a critical tool for projecting future changes in benthic ecosystem metabolism and regional carbon budgets.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying a terrestrial-derived metabolic scaling and stoichiometric framework—used to model plant-soil carbon transfer—to the marine benthic-pelagic interface, explicitly treating the water column as a processing system that modifies export quality.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Future of Arctic Marine Primary Production: A Mechanistic Model of Phytoplankton Response to Accelerated Spring Sea Ice Retreat
proposals.motivation: The Arctic is warming at more than twice the global average rate, leading to a dramatic, well-documented decline in spring sea ice extent and thickness. This ice loss fundamentally alters the underwater light field and stratification, but the net impact on pan-Arctic annual primary production remains uncertain, with competing models suggesting potential increases from longer open-water seasons or limitations from nutrient drawdown and altered phenology. A mechanistic, process-based projection is urgently needed to inform predictions of future carbon cycling and ecosystem services in this rapidly changing region.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that continued spring sea ice loss will lead to a non-linear increase in pan-Arctic annual primary production, but that this increase will be regionally heterogeneous and ultimately constrained by the availability of bioavailable iron and the efficiency of the microbial loop in recycling nutrients.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution (4 km) regional ocean-biogeochemical model (e.g., MITgcm or ROMS coupled to a biogeochemical module like BEC) configured for the Arctic. The model will be forced with future climate scenarios (SSP5-8.5) from CMIP6 models to project ice conditions to 2050. Key innovations include the explicit representation of under-ice and marginal ice zone phytoplankton blooms based on observed light thresholds, dynamic iron cycling from sedimentary and riverine sources, and nitrification processes to account for ammonium recycling. Model skill will be validated against satellite-derived chlorophyll and in-situ biogeochemical data from the 2003-2021 period.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first pan-Arctic, decadal-scale projection of primary production that mechanistically integrates ice retreat physics with iron-limitation dynamics and microbial nutrient recycling. It will quantify the relative importance of light versus nutrient limitation under future conditions and identify potential 'hotspots' and 'coldspots' of future productivity change, directly informing international assessments like the IPCC.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have correlated ice loss with bloom timing, this proposal uniquely combines high-resolution ice-ocean physics with advanced biogeochemistry (iron, nitrification) to move beyond correlation to a causal, process-based future projection, directly testing the interplay between extended growth seasons and nutrient constraints.

proposals.title: A Scale-Aware Validation of Satellite Ocean Primary Production Using a Data-Assimilating, Eddy-Permitting Biogeochemical Model Constrained by BGC-Argo Floats
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived estimates of net primary production (NPP) are critical for assessing ocean carbon cycling and ecosystem responses to climate change, yet their validation remains a major challenge. Traditional validation relies on sparse ship-based measurements, creating a scale mismatch with satellite pixels and leaving vast ocean regions, especially seasonally ice-covered seas, poorly assessed. The expanding global array of Biogeochemical-Argo (BGC-Argo) floats provides an unprecedented, year-round source of vertically resolved in situ data to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: A three-dimensional, data-assimilating biogeochemical model, continuously updated with BGC-Argo profiles, can generate gap-free, physically consistent NPP fields that, when rigorously aggregated to satellite scales, provide a more robust and statistically significant validation benchmark than direct point-to-pixel comparisons.
proposals.method: We will implement a regional or basin-scale configuration of a coupled physical-biogeochemical model (e.g., MITgcm or ROMS coupled to a nutrient-phytoplankton-zooplankton-detritus model) at eddy-permitting resolution (~3-5 km). BGC-Argo profiles of chlorophyll, nitrate, and backscatter will be assimilated using an ensemble Kalman filter to update model state variables. The model's daily, 3D NPP fields will be integrated to surface values and then aggregated spatially and temporally to precisely match the footprint and overpass time of specific satellite sensors (e.g., MODIS, VIIRS). Statistical metrics (bias, RMSE, correlation) will compare aggregated model NPP to contemporaneous satellite NPP products.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scale-aware validation framework that directly addresses the spatial-temporal mismatch problem in satellite ocean color validation. It will deliver a quantified uncertainty assessment for satellite NPP products across ocean basins, with particular insight into high-latitude and marginal ice zone regions where Arrigo's expertise is focused. The resulting validated, gap-free model fields will also serve as a valuable tool for studying bloom dynamics and carbon export.
proposals.novelty: While BGC-Argo data and models have been used separately, this approach uniquely integrates them via data assimilation specifically to create a satellite-scale validation target, moving beyond simple match-up analysis. The eddy-permitting resolution is critical for accurately representing frontal processes that control subduction and export, themes evident in the researcher's recent work.

proposals.title: Validating Satellite Primary Production Algorithms in the Pan-Arctic Seasonal Ice Zone Using Autonomous Biogeochemical Argo Float Observations
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived estimates of net primary production (NPP) are critical for assessing carbon cycling and ecosystem responses to rapid Arctic change. However, existing NPP models, like the Vertically Generalized Production Model (VGPM), are poorly validated in high-latitude, seasonally ice-free waters due to a scarcity of in situ data, particularly regarding subsurface biomass and photoacclimation. This limits our confidence in pan-Arctic carbon budget assessments and trend analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that autonomous biogeochemical Argo (BGC-Argo) float measurements of chlorophyll fluorescence, particulate backscatter, and irradiance profiles will reveal systematic biases in satellite NPP algorithms in the Arctic, primarily due to errors in representing subsurface chlorophyll maxima and non-photochemical quenching during high-light conditions.
proposals.method: We will deploy a fleet of BGC-Argo floats equipped with sensors for chlorophyll fluorescence, backscattering, PAR, nitrate, and oxygen in the Chukchi and Barents Seas during the seasonal ice retreat. Float-derived NPP will be calculated using bio-optical profiles and a depth-resolved model. Concurrent satellite NPP from standard algorithms (VGPM, Carbon-based Productivity Model) will be extracted and co-located. Statistical comparison (root mean square error, bias analysis) will quantify discrepancies and identify environmental drivers (e.g., surface stratification, mixed layer depth) of model error using multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first pan-Arctic, seasonally resolved validation dataset for satellite NPP from an autonomous platform, directly addressing a key uncertainty in polar carbon cycling. It will produce regionally tuned algorithm coefficients to improve the accuracy of Arctic primary production estimates in climate models and remote sensing products.
proposals.novelty: While BGC-Argo floats have been used globally, their systematic application for validating satellite NPP algorithms in the dynamic and data-sparse Arctic marginal ice zone is novel. This approach moves beyond ship-based snapshot validation to capture the full seasonal progression of production and its subsurface drivers.

proposals.title: Quantifying Functional Group Contributions to Arctic Primary Production Shifts Using a Taxon-Specific eDNA Marker Framework
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is experiencing rapid, climate-driven changes in sea ice and light availability, leading to documented shifts in phytoplankton bloom phenology and magnitude. However, current satellite and bulk production metrics cannot attribute production increases to specific phytoplankton functional groups, hindering predictions of future carbon export and ecosystem function. A method to directly link taxonomic shifts to functional output is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observed increases in pan-Arctic primary production are disproportionately driven by specific, fast-responding phytoplankton functional groups (e.g., diatoms, Phaeocystis), and that their relative contribution can be quantified using a novel panel of taxon-specific genetic markers.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Microbial Source Tracking (MST) concept by developing a quantitative droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) panel for key Arctic phytoplankton functional groups (diatoms, dinoflagellates, Phaeocystis, diazotrophs) using curated, taxon-specific genetic markers from pre-2026 databases (e.g., 18S/16S rRNA, *rbcL*). This panel will be applied to archived DNA from time-series samples (e.g., from the Distributed Biological Observatory) and concurrent mooring data. Shifts in marker abundance will be statistically correlated with independent, coincident measurements of primary production from satellite-derived chlorophyll-a (MODIS/VIIRS algorithms) and in situ 14C uptake assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond descriptive community ecology to provide a mechanistic, quantitative link between phytoplankton group dynamics and carbon production. It will deliver a novel molecular toolkit for attributing biogeochemical change to specific taxa and improve the parameterization of functional groups in biogeochemical models for the high-latitude oceans.
proposals.novelty: The novel adaptation of the MST framework from fecal pollution tracking to phytoplankton functional ecology provides a targeted, quantitative alternative to general metabarcoding. This allows for direct hypothesis testing about group-specific production, which correlative community data alone cannot achieve.

proposals.title: Decoding the Genomic Signature of Arctic Phytoplankton Bloom Succession: A High-Resolution eDNA Metabarcoding Study of the Chukchi Sea
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is experiencing rapid climate-driven changes, including sea ice loss and altered nutrient regimes, leading to shifts in phytoplankton bloom phenology and community composition. These shifts, from ice-associated diatoms to later-season flagellates, have profound implications for carbon export efficiency and food web dynamics, yet traditional microscopy and pigment-based methods lack the resolution to track fine-scale, concurrent taxonomic changes during bloom progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed increases in net primary production in the warming Chukchi Sea are driven by a predictable, climate-forced succession in phytoplankton community structure, which can be resolved at high taxonomic resolution using environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding.
proposals.method: We will apply high-throughput eDNA metabarcoding targeting the 18S rRNA V4 and 16S rRNA (for cyanobacteria) gene regions to archived water samples from the Chukchi Sea, collected across multiple bloom phases from 2017-2022. Sequence data will be processed using the DADA2 pipeline for exact amplicon sequence variant (ASV) inference, followed by taxonomic assignment against the PR2 database. Community shifts will be correlated with concurrent in-situ measurements of production (14C uptake), nutrients, light, and physical data (e.g., frontal subduction metrics) using multivariate statistical analyses (PERMANOVA, RDA) in R.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, DNA-based taxonomic timeline of phytoplankton succession in the climatically sensitive Chukchi Sea, directly linking community shifts to carbon export events. It will establish a genetic baseline for future monitoring and improve mechanistic models of how specific phytoplankton groups respond to and mediate biogeochemical change in a warming Arctic.
proposals.novelty: While Arrigo's prior work quantified bloom frequency and carbon export physically, this proposal novelly integrates genetic metabarcoding to identify the specific taxonomic drivers behind those physical and biogeochemical patterns, moving from correlation to mechanistic understanding of community-level responses.

proposals.title: Inverting Ocean Color Spectra to Estimate Subsurface Phytoplankton Biomass Profiles: A Geophysical-Inspired Optimization Approach for the Changing Arctic
proposals.motivation: Satellite ocean color provides only a surface-weighted estimate of phytoplankton biomass, obscuring critical subsurface chlorophyll maxima (SCMs) that dominate Arctic primary production, especially under ice. Quantifying the vertical structure of phytoplankton is essential for accurate estimates of carbon export and ecosystem response to rapid climate-driven changes in light and stratification. Current empirical methods for retrieving depth-resolved chlorophyll are limited and lack a robust physical-optical foundation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bio-optical model, when coupled with an iterative geophysical-style inversion algorithm, can accurately retrieve the vertical profile of phytoplankton biomass from spectral water-leaving radiance, particularly for optically complex Arctic waters with strong SCMs.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Bayesian or gradient-based seismic inversion framework. The forward model will be a layered bio-optical model (e.g., based on inherent optical properties and two-stream approximations) that simulates remote-sensing reflectance from a given chlorophyll profile.
