Using measured hyperspectral data (e.g., from field campaigns or HICO), we will employ an optimization algorithm (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt or a Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampler) to iteratively adjust the chlorophyll profile to minimize the misfit between modeled and observed spectra, constrained by prior knowledge of typical Arctic profile shapes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, physics-based method to derive 3D phytoplankton fields from 2D satellite imagery, directly addressing a key uncertainty in Arctic carbon cycling. It will provide a new tool to re-analyze historical ocean color records to study the temporal evolution of SCMs, offering critical insights into how subsurface blooms respond to sea-ice loss and altered nutrient regimes.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly framing ocean color inversion as a geophysical inverse problem, moving beyond simple empirical regressions. It integrates rigorous optimization techniques from seismology with Arctic-specific bio-optical models to solve for a continuous vertical profile, a significant advance over layer-based or statistical methods prevalent before 2026.

proposals.title: Quantitative Reconstruction of Past Sea Ice Dynamics in the Arctic Ocean Using an Inverse Model of Phytoplankton Production Proxies
proposals.motivation: Reconstructing past sea ice extent is critical for understanding Arctic climate sensitivity, but direct paleo proxies for ice cover are sparse and often qualitative. Concurrently, sediment cores provide high-resolution records of past phytoplankton production, which is strongly modulated by light availability under ice. This creates an opportunity to use well-resolved biological data to infer physical conditions, addressing a key gap in paleoceanographic records of the high-latitude ocean.
proposals.hypothesis: The temporal record of primary production derived from sediment cores can be modeled as the convolution of a solar/nutrient forcing driver with an ice-cover attenuation filter, allowing the ice history to be quantitatively recovered by solving the corresponding inverse problem.
proposals.method: We will formulate a forward model where Production(t) = Driver(t) * (1 - α * IceIndex(t)), with Driver(t) estimated from orbital insolation and paleo-nutrient proxies (e.g., diatom-bound N isotopes). Using high-resolution biogenic silica or chlorin records from the Chukchi Sea as Production(t), we will solve the linear inverse problem for IceIndex(t) using Tikhonov regularization to ensure temporal smoothness, implemented via the L-curve method in a MATLAB or Python optimization framework. Uncertainty will be assessed via Monte Carlo simulation perturbing driver estimates and core chronologies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, quantitative sea ice reconstruction for the Holocene Arctic, directly linking biological and physical change. It will provide a new methodological framework for interpreting sediment core data, moving beyond correlation to a causal, model-based inference of ice cover from production trends.
proposals.novelty: This approach adapts formal inverse theory from geophysics (seismic deconvolution) to paleoceanography, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary analogy. It treats ice cover as a solvable filter rather than a correlated environmental variable, offering a mechanistic and quantitative alternative to existing qualitative ice proxies.

proposals.title: Constraining the Phytoplankton-Mixed Layer-Ice Feedback in the High Arctic Using a Bayesian Inversion of Sedimentary Biomarker Records
proposals.motivation: Arctic sea ice decline is accelerating, but climate models struggle to represent key feedbacks. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Arrigo's work on under-ice blooms, shows phytoplankton can modulate the mixed layer through light absorption and carbon export, potentially feeding back on ice formation. Quantifying this biologically mediated feedback's historical strength is critical for improving projections of future Arctic change.
proposals.hypothesis: High-resolution sedimentary records of primary production and phytoplankton community structure can be used to inversely model not only past sea ice conditions but also biologically driven changes in mixed layer depth, revealing a dynamic feedback loop that amplified or dampened ice cover changes across late Holocene climate transitions.
proposals.method: We will compile a pan-Arctic sediment core dataset with proxies for total production (chlorin, biogenic silica), algal groups (IP25, HBI IIIa, brassicasterol), and export (230Th-normalized fluxes). A process-informed Bayesian hierarchical model will be developed, integrating established relationships (e.g., IP25-sea ice) with a simplified 1D bio-optical/physical module. The inversion will treat MLD as a latent variable influenced by algal biomass and type, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to constrain posterior distributions of past ice cover, MLD, and feedback strength during defined climatic intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative estimates of the strength and sign of the phytoplankton-mixed layer-ice feedback from paleo-archives. It will deliver a novel, transferable inversion framework for coupled bio-physical paleo-system analysis and provide critical empirical constraints to validate and improve the biological components of Arctic climate models.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond using sedimentary proxies as passive recorders of ice to actively diagnosing a coupled biological-physical feedback mechanism. The inversion framework explicitly tests for phytoplankton's role as a dynamic modulator of its own environment, a hypothesis suggested but not quantitatively constrained from paleo-records.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Pan-Arctic Sea Ice History Over the Past Millennium Using Sedimentary Pigment Records and an Inverse Modeling Framework
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived records of Arctic sea ice cover are limited to the past ~40 years, providing an insufficient baseline to contextualize recent rapid ice loss. Paleo-reconstructions of sea ice, primarily from biomarker proxies like IP25, are spatially and temporally sparse. This project addresses the critical need for longer-term, high-resolution sea ice histories by leveraging more abundant sedimentary records of primary production, which are intrinsically linked to ice conditions through light availability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal record of primary production, reconstructed from sedimentary chlorophyll and carotenoid pigments in strategically selected Arctic cores, can be inverted using a coupled ice-ocean-biogeochemical model to quantitatively reconstruct past sea ice concentration and seasonality.
proposals.method: We will compile a new database of sedimentary pigment records from Arctic shelf and basin sediments, focusing on regions with strong ice-productivity coupling. A 1-D vertical model, incorporating pre-2026 representations of ice optics, snow cover, and phytoplankton photoacclimation, will be developed to simulate under-ice light fields and resultant primary production. The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) framework to estimate the most probable historical ice conditions (thickness, concentration, melt timing) that produced the observed sediment production trends, rigorously quantifying uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, multi-century reconstruction of Arctic sea ice variability derived from biological archives, directly complementing physical proxy records. It will advance the mechanistic understanding of ice-ecosystem linkages and provide a critical validation target for climate models simulating past and future Arctic change.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has used models to predict production from ice data, this study innovatively inverts that relationship. It moves beyond correlation to establish a causative, mechanistic framework for inferring a physical environmental driver (ice) from a biological response (production), a novel application of inverse methods in paleoceanography.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of a Rapid Arctic Sea Ice Recovery on Annual Net Primary Production: A Coupled Biogeochemical-Ice Model Scenario
proposals.motivation: Arctic marine primary production has increased significantly over recent decades, largely driven by sea ice loss and extended open-water periods. While projections generally favor continued ice decline, natural variability and potential climate feedbacks introduce uncertainty, including the possibility of rapid, multi-year ice recovery. Quantifying the biological impact of such a reversal is critical for understanding the resilience of Arctic ecosystems and the stability of carbon export pathways in a non-linear climate system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rapid, sustained recovery of Arctic sea ice extent and thickness would lead to a disproportionately large reduction in annual net primary production (NPP) compared to the linear gains observed during ice loss, due to the re-establishment of light limitation and the suppression of under-ice and marginal ice zone blooms.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution regional ocean biogeochemical model (e.g., MITgcm or ROMS coupled to a biogeochemical module like BEC) with sophisticated sea ice and spectral light modules. The model will be forced with atmospheric reanalysis data (2003-2021) but with sea ice concentration and thickness fields artificially nudged towards a recovered state (e.g., 1980s climatology) over a 5-year period. Key analyses will focus on changes in the phenology, magnitude, and vertical distribution of primary production, specifically partitioning losses between under-ice, marginal ice zone, and open-water blooms, informed by Arrigo's prior pan-Arctic bloom frequency analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative estimate of primary production sensitivity to a sea ice recovery scenario, offering a crucial counterpoint to studies focused on ice loss. It will improve understanding of ecosystem hysteresis and identify which bloom types (under-ice vs. MIZ) and regions (e.g., Chukchi vs. Barents Seas) are most vulnerable to ice regime shifts, directly informing projections of Arctic carbon cycling and fisheries productivity.
proposals.novelty: While extensive modeling work exists on production gains from ice loss, systematically modeling the biological consequences of a hypothetical rapid ice recovery—a plausible but understudied scenario—is novel. It uniquely applies advanced, validated ice-ocean-biogeochemical coupling to explore ecosystem reversibility rather than linear change.

proposals.title: Modeling Arctic Marine Ecosystem Resilience to a Trophic Regime Shift: Salp vs. Krill Dominance Under Changing Sea Ice and Iron Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Antarctic krill are a well-established central trophic node, but climate-driven changes in the Arctic, including sea ice retreat and altered nutrient regimes, may favor alternative zooplankton like salps. Salps have different grazing efficiencies, carbon export mechanisms, and iron recycling capabilities, which could fundamentally alter ecosystem structure, carbon sequestration, and higher trophic level productivity. This shift is critical to model now, as observed changes in under-ice bloom phenology and frontal dynamics in regions like the Chukchi Sea may already be altering zooplankton community composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a climate-forced shift from a krill-dominated to a salp-dominated Arctic pelagic ecosystem will reduce overall ecosystem resilience, characterized by lower energy transfer to vertebrates and altered magnitude and stoichiometry of vertical carbon export.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled 1D physical-biological-ecological model for the Pacific Arctic sector, building on frameworks like the Regional Ocean Modeling System (ROMS) coupled to a Nutrient-Phytoplankton-Zooplankton-Detritus (NPZD) model. The model will be uniquely parameterized with two distinct zooplankton functional types (krill vs. salps) with differing grazing, growth, mortality, and fecal pellet export rates derived from pre-2026 literature. Key simulations will force the system with IPCC CMIP6 climate projections (SSP5-8.5) to 2050, with a focus on sea ice loss and potential changes in iron bioavailability, to test ecosystem stability and carbon flux under competing zooplankton regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework to project Arctic ecosystem trajectories beyond the canonical krill-centric model. It will directly inform predictions of future fishery yields and carbon sequestration potential in a warming Arctic, bridging the gap between phytoplankton bloom studies and higher trophic level ecology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped bloom phenology, this proposal uniquely integrates the trophic consequences of a specific zooplankton regime shift with the physical and biogeochemical drivers (sea ice, iron) central to Arrigo's recent research, moving from correlation to mechanistic causation.

proposals.title: Regional Divergence in *Phaeocystis antarctica* Populations: Linking Genomic Variation to Iron and Light Regimes Across the Southern Ocean
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean is a mosaic of distinct biogeochemical provinces with varying iron and light availability, which are primary drivers of phytoplankton community structure. Climate-driven changes in sea ice, mixed layer depth, and iron supply are altering these regimes non-uniformly, creating a natural experiment in population-level adaptation. Understanding how key phytoplankton species like *Phaeocystis antarctica* diverge regionally is critical for predicting future biogeochemical function and carbon export in this high-latitude ecosystem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that populations of *Phaeocystis antarctica* in the iron-limited, seasonally ice-free Ross Sea have diverged genomically and physiologically from populations in the iron-enriched, marginal ice zone of the western Antarctic Peninsula, and that these adaptations are linked to distinct strategies for optimizing growth under contrasting iron and light regimes.
proposals.method: We will conduct targeted field campaigns in the Ross Sea and along the western Antarctic Peninsula during the spring bloom. Using a combination of metagenomic and metatranscriptomic sequencing of size-fractionated samples, we will identify single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and gene expression profiles specific to *P. antarctica* populations. These genomic data will be linked to in situ physiological measurements (Fast Repetition Rate fluorometry for photophysiology, trace metal-clean sampling for cellular iron quotas) and regional physical-biogeochemical conditions derived from satellite data (MODIS, AMSR2) and autonomous glider profiles of irradiance and chlorophyll.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, regionally comparative evidence of genomic divergence in a dominant Southern Ocean phytoplankton species linked to environmental drivers. It will advance our understanding of phytoplankton adaptive capacity and establish a framework for predicting how population-level changes may alter future bloom phenology and carbon export efficiency in a warming climate.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented regional bloom patterns, this study moves beyond community composition to explicitly test for intra-specific genomic and physiological divergence in response to the heterogeneous climate forcing across the Southern Ocean, a scale of adaptation rarely examined in polar phytoplankton.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Drivers of Polar Pelagic Range Shifts: Quantifying the Coupled Influence of Light, Phytoplankton Phenology, and Temperature on Krill and Finfish Latitudinal Distributions
proposals.motivation: Rapid Arctic warming is altering the physical and biogeochemical seascape, with documented shifts in phytoplankton bloom timing, extent, and vertical carbon export. The response of higher trophic levels, particularly key zooplankton like krill and forage fish, to these coupled changes remains poorly quantified, creating a critical gap in predicting future polar ecosystem structure and carbon flow. This work directly addresses how bottom-up (light, productivity) and top-down (thermal habitat) constraints interact to govern species' latitudinal ranges.
proposals.hypothesis: Future latitudinal range shifts of key krill and finfish species in the Arctic and Subarctic will be non-linearly constrained not only by thermal isotherms but by the spatiotemporal match (or mismatch) with under-ice and marginal ice zone phytoplankton blooms, which are themselves modulated by sea ice retreat and nitrification/iron supply.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite-derived sea ice, chlorophyll-a, and sea surface temperature data (2003-2025) with a mechanistic, trait-based ecological niche model. The model will parameterize species-specific thermal tolerances and food requirements based on pre-2026 literature. Forcing fields will include projected changes in bloom frequency (from Arrigo's prior pan-Arctic analysis) and frontal dynamics influencing carbon export. Range shift projections will be generated using an ensemble modeling approach (e.g., Biomod2 in R) under multiple CMIP6 climate scenarios, explicitly testing the relative contribution of temperature versus phenology drivers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a process-based, quantitative framework for predicting pelagic range shifts that moves beyond simple thermal envelope models by integrating critical bottom-up biogeochemical drivers. It will deliver novel maps of potential future habitat for key indicator species under varying ice-ocean-biogeochemistry scenarios, directly informing ecosystem-based management in a changing Arctic.
proposals.novelty: While range shifts are widely studied, this proposal uniquely focuses on the mechanistic link between specific, high-latitude phytoplankton bloom types (under-ice vs. MIZ) documented in recent work and higher trophic level distributions, explicitly modeling how changes in bloom timing and location driven by physical fronts and nutrient regimes constrain animal movement.

proposals.title: Projecting the Poleward Migration of Arctic Phytoplankton and Microzooplankton Communities Under a Shifting Climate Regime
proposals.motivation: Rapid Arctic warming is fundamentally altering the physical and biogeochemical seascape, with documented shifts in sea ice extent, light availability, and nutrient regimes. These changes are expected to drive a southward displacement of oceanographic conditions, yet the consequences for the distribution, composition, and phenology of foundational lower trophic levels (phytoplankton and microzooplankton) remain poorly quantified. Understanding these shifts is critical for predicting future Arctic marine ecosystem structure, carbon export, and fisheries productivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that projected changes in sea ice, mixed layer depth, and nutrient supply will drive a significant poleward migration of key phytoplankton functional types and their microzooplankton grazers, with the rate and magnitude of this shift modulated by regional differences in iron and nitrogen availability.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution (5 km) regional ocean-biogeochemical model (e.g., MITgcm or ROMS coupled to a plankton functional type module like BLING or COBALT) forced by downscaled CMIP6 SSP5-8.5 scenario outputs for 2025-2050. The model will be initialized and validated using pre-2026 satellite-derived chlorophyll, in-situ phytoplankton community data, and zooplankton net tow observations from the Arctic. Lagrangian particle tracking will be implemented to simulate the advection and environmental forcing experienced by plankton communities, quantifying their projected displacement vectors and identifying frontal zones acting as biogeographic barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pan-Arctic, high-resolution projections of plankton community redistribution, explicitly linking physical drivers to biogeographic shifts. It will quantify the future frequency and location of under-ice and marginal ice zone blooms, directly extending Arrigo's prior observational analyses. The results will provide a critical mechanistic framework for anticipating changes in Arctic carbon cycling and food web dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled bulk chlorophyll changes, this proposal uniquely integrates phytoplankton functional types, microzooplankton grazers, and Lagrangian tracking within a high-resolution Arctic domain to project community-level biogeographic shifts, moving beyond simple biomass trends to forecast ecosystem reorganization.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Poleward Phytoplankton Community Shifts on Carbon Export Efficiency in the Changing Arctic Ocean
proposals.motivation: Rapid Arctic warming is driving a poleward expansion of temperate phytoplankton species and altering bloom phenology, as evidenced by increased under-ice and marginal ice zone blooms. The biogeochemical consequences of these community shifts, particularly for the biological carbon pump, remain poorly quantified at the basin scale. This gap limits our ability to predict feedbacks between Arctic ecosystem change and the global carbon cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the poleward shift in phytoplankton community composition, characterized by an increasing prevalence of smaller, less-ballasted species, will lead to a significant basin-scale reduction in carbon export efficiency, despite potential increases in net primary production.
proposals.method: We will employ a regional ocean biogeochemical model (e.g., MITgcm or ROMS coupled to a biogeochemical module like BEC) for the pan-Arctic domain. The model will be forced with IPCC CMIP6 future climate scenarios (SSP5-8.5). Key innovations include: 1) parameterizing functional groups (e.g., diatoms, Phaeocystis, small flagellates) with observed, group-specific carbon export efficiencies derived from prior field studies; 2) implementing a dynamic trait-based module where species dominance shifts in response to light, stratification, and nutrient (nitrate, iron) regimes; and 3) explicitly tracking vertical carbon export via sinking particles and subduction, validated against existing sediment trap and BGC-Argo float data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first basin-scale, mechanistic projection of how phytoplankton range shifts directly modulate Arctic carbon sequestration. It will quantify the potential decoupling between primary production and export, providing a critical constraint for Earth System Models. The results will inform climate policy by refining projections of the Arctic's future role as a carbon sink or source.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped bloom patterns or studied export at specific sites, this proposal uniquely integrates observed species-specific export traits into a dynamic, high-resolution model to directly link community ecology shifts to basin-scale carbon cycle feedbacks.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Historical Sea-Ice Dynamics in the Pacific Arctic Using Diatom Photophysiological and Morphological Adaptations
proposals.motivation: Satellite-derived sea-ice records are limited to the past ~45 years, providing an incomplete baseline for understanding Arctic climate change. Paleo-proxies from sediment cores offer longer records but are spatially and temporally discontinuous. Extant phytoplankton populations, shaped by evolutionary pressures, may encode a continuous, high-resolution history of past ice conditions in their functional traits, offering a novel complement to existing methods.
proposals.hypothesis: That quantifiable photophysiological traits (e.g., photoacclimation parameters, ice-algal pigment signatures) and morphological adaptations (e.g., chain length, frustule silicification) in dominant Arctic diatoms are correlated with modern sea-ice regimes and can be used to statistically infer past ice concentration and bloom phenology.
proposals.method: We will isolate key diatom species (e.g., *Fragilariopsis cylindrus*, *Melosira arctica*) from under-ice, marginal ice zone (MIZ), and open-water regimes in the Chukchi and Beaufort Seas. Cultured strains will be characterized using Pulse-Amplitude Modulated (PAM) fluorometry to derive photophysiological parameters (α, Ek, Fv/Fm). Morphological traits will be quantified via scanning electron microscopy. A multivariate statistical model (e.g., Random Forest regression) will be trained to correlate these trait suites with satellite-derived sea-ice concentration (2003-2025) and bloom timing data. This trained model will then be applied to trait data from contemporary sediment trap and surface sediment samples to hindcast local ice history.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a new biological proxy for sea ice, directly linking modern ecological genomics/physiology with paleo-reconstruction. It will provide spatially explicit, century-scale hindcasts of ice dynamics in critical regions like the Chukchi Sea, informing models of carbon export and ecosystem response. The approach bridges Dr. Arrigo's expertise in Arctic phytoplankton ecology, bloom dynamics, and biogeochemical cycles.
proposals.novelty: While genetic markers have been proposed for biogeography, using quantifiable, physiological trait suites of extant phytoplankton as a calibrated inverse model for past physical conditions is a novel synthesis of ecology and paleoceanography. It moves beyond species presence/absence to leverage functional adaptation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Efficiency and Pathways of Carbon Export from Under-Ice Phytoplankton Blooms in the Marginal Ice Zone of the Pacific Arctic
proposals.motivation: Satellite and in-situ studies (including Arrigo et al., 2014; 2023) have established that under-ice phytoplankton blooms (UIBs) are a significant, recurrent feature in the Arctic, contributing substantially to annual primary production. However, the fate of this carbon—whether it is recycled in surface waters, exported to depth, or advected laterally—remains poorly quantified, creating a critical gap in understanding the Arctic Ocean's biological carbon pump under rapid sea-ice decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that carbon fixed by UIBs in the marginal ice zone (MIZ) is exported with high efficiency via the subduction of phytoplankton-rich surface waters at frontal boundaries and the rapid sinking of aggregated cells, making a disproportionately large contribution to carbon sequestration relative to open-water blooms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a targeted field campaign in the Chukchi Sea MIZ during the UIB period, deploying a suite of sensors on AUVs and moorings to map bloom distribution and physical structure. Carbon export will be quantified using surface-tethered sediment traps, thorium-234 deficit measurements, and optical backscatter sensors. We will employ a Lagrangian particle-tracking model, initialized with in-situ velocity and particle data, to simulate the three-dimensional advective pathways and subduction of bloom-derived carbon at observed frontal features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, process-based budget for UIB carbon export in the Pacific Arctic, directly linking physical dynamics to biological carbon flux. It will deliver a mechanistic framework to improve the representation of ice-associated carbon export in regional and global biogeochemical models under future climate scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented UIB occurrence and some export events, this study uniquely integrates high-resolution physical oceanography (frontal subduction) with advanced biogeochemical tracers and Lagrangian modeling to trace the complete fate of UIB carbon from fixation to potential sequestration.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Biological Carbon Pump in the Changing Arctic: Quantifying Export Flux from Revised Under-Ice Primary Production Estimates
proposals.motivation: Recent analyses, including Arrigo's 2021 pan-Arctic study, demonstrate that under-ice phytoplankton blooms are more frequent and productive than previously assumed. This revised primary production (PP) necessitates a re-evaluation of the Arctic Ocean's carbon sequestration potential, as the efficiency of the biological carbon pump—the transfer of this carbon to depth—remains poorly constrained. Quantifying this export is critical for improving climate models, as the Arctic's role as a carbon sink is a key uncertainty in global biogeochemical cycles under rapid climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the carbon export efficiency (e-ratio) associated with under-ice and marginal ice zone phytoplankton blooms is systematically different from that of open-water blooms, and that incorporating these revised PP estimates and their specific export pathways will lead to a significant upward revision of the Arctic's total carbon sequestration flux.
proposals.method: We will synthesize satellite-derived PP estimates (using algorithms like the vertically generalized production model) with in situ data from Arctic cruises (including Arrigo's Chukchi Sea work) to create a revised PP climatology. Carbon export will be quantified by applying region- and bloom-type-specific e-ratios derived from sediment trap data and the 234Thorium deficit method. A key focus will be modeling the physical export pathways, such as frontal subduction identified in the Chukchi Sea, using high-resolution regional ocean models (e.g., MITgcm) coupled with Lagrangian particle tracking to simulate the fate of bloom-derived carbon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first pan-Arctic estimate of carbon export explicitly based on the new paradigm of significant under-ice productivity. It will deliver updated, mechanistically informed carbon flux parameterizations for Earth System Models. The results will directly constrain the Arctic's contemporary carbon sink strength and improve projections of its evolution in a seasonally ice-free future.
proposals.novelty: While recent work has revised Arctic PP estimates, a systematic, mechanistic quantification of the consequent carbon export—particularly from under-ice blooms and via physical processes like subduction—has not been undertaken. This proposal uniquely integrates the revised PP fields with specific physical export mechanisms to close the carbon budget.

proposals.title: Quantifying Net Radiative Forcing from Coupled Nitrogen-Carbon-N2O Dynamics in a Warming Arctic Ocean
proposals.motivation: The Arctic Ocean is experiencing rapid warming and sea ice loss, driving significant shifts in phytoplankton bloom phenology and biogeochemistry. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Arrigo's work on under-ice blooms, highlights the critical role of nitrogen (N) availability in controlling carbon (C) export and the production of nitrous oxide (N2O), a potent greenhouse gas. However, the net climate impact of these linked N-C-N2O feedbacks remains unquantified, creating a major uncertainty in projecting the Arctic's future role in the global climate system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increased primary production and carbon export from expanding Arctic phytoplankton blooms will be partially offset by enhanced N2O production from associated nitrification and denitrification processes, significantly modulating the net radiative forcing of the region.
proposals.method: We will integrate a 1-D biogeochemical model (configured for the Chukchi Sea) with a radiative forcing module. The core model will couple established representations of algal growth (based on light, iron, and nitrate), carbon export (via a ballast parameterization), and N2O production (from nitrifier-denitrification and hydroxylamine oxidation pathways). We will force the model with pre-2026 satellite-derived ice cover and light fields, and run sensitivity experiments under projected warming scenarios to quantify the C-sequestration and N2O-emission trade-off. The net radiative forcing will be calculated using established IPCC AR5 global warming potentials.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative estimate of net climate forcing from coupled N-C-N2O dynamics in the Arctic marine system. It will directly inform climate models by providing a mechanistic framework and constrained parameters for a key biogeochemical feedback. The results will clarify whether the Arctic Ocean's biological pump will act as a net climate mitigator or contributor under future change.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined Arctic blooms or N2O fluxes in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Arrigo's expertise in high-latitude phytoplankton dynamics with the climate-active N2O cycle to solve for the net radiative effect, a critical synthesis not yet attempted.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Trophic Link Between Polynyas and Penguins: A Mechanistic Analysis of Primary Production and Adélie Penguin Colony Dynamics in the Ross Sea
proposals.motivation: The Ross Sea Polynya is one of the most productive regions in the Southern Ocean, supporting a rich food web culminating in apex predators like Adélie penguins. However, the quantitative, mechanistic links between polynya-scale primary production, its interannual variability driven by physical forcing, and the demographic health of penguin colonies remain poorly constrained. Understanding this trophic connection is critical for predicting how climate-driven changes in sea ice, polynya dynamics, and phytoplankton phenology will impact polar marine ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: Interannual variability in the magnitude and timing of polynya primary production, particularly the export of carbon to depth via processes like frontal subduction, is a primary determinant of krill availability and subsequent Adélie penguin breeding success and colony size.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite-derived sea ice concentration, chlorophyll-a, and sea surface temperature (2003-2025) to characterize polynya extent, bloom timing, and net primary production (NPP) using established bio-optical models. These data will be coupled with in-situ carbon export estimates from historical Arrigo-led campaigns and ocean reanalysis data to model prey-field (krill) accessibility. Penguin colony metrics (population counts, breeding success from long-term monitoring) will be analyzed against the physical-biological time series using structural equation modeling (SEM) to disentangle causal pathways and quantify the relative influence of production magnitude versus phenology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, quantitative framework linking polynya physics and biogeochemistry directly to Adélie penguin population dynamics. It will identify which characteristics of polynya production (e.g., bloom initiation, peak biomass, export flux) are most critical for predator success, offering a predictive tool for assessing colony vulnerability under future climate scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While past studies correlate broad-scale productivity with predator presence, this proposal uniquely focuses on the causal chain from physical polynya processes to carbon export and finally to penguin demography, moving beyond correlation to mechanism by integrating the researcher's expertise in vertical carbon export and under-ice blooms with top predator ecology.

proposals.title: Quantifying Wind, Meltwater, and Circulation Drivers of Interannual Polynya Variability and Primary Production in the Ross and Chukchi Seas
proposals.motivation: Polynyas are critical sites of intense phytoplankton blooms and carbon export in the high Arctic and Antarctic, but their interannual variability is poorly understood. Pre-2026 observations, including Arrigo's work, show high variability in bloom frequency and timing linked to polynya dynamics. Understanding the physical drivers of polynya area and duration is essential to predict how climate-driven changes in winds, sea ice melt, and circulation will impact polar marine ecosystems and biogeochemical cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interannual variability in polynya area and phytoplankton production is primarily controlled by a combination of offshore wind stress (creating latent heat polynyas), coastal meltwater discharge (stabilizing the water column), and mesoscale circulation features (influencing nutrient supply and bloom retention).
proposals.method: We will employ the Regional Ocean Modeling System (ROMS) coupled to a biogeochemical model (e.g., CoSiNE or Fennel-type NPZD) at ~5 km resolution for the Ross and Chukchi Sea domains. A series of sensitivity experiments (2003-2021) will isolate the effects of variable wind forcing (from ERA5), prescribed meltwater runoff (from RACMO/regional climate models), and altered boundary currents. Model validation will utilize satellite-derived polynya area (AMSR-E/AMSR2), chlorophyll-a (MODIS/VIIRS), and in-situ carbon export data from prior field campaigns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, quantitative framework linking specific physical forcings to polynya formation and biological productivity. It will directly inform projections of future polar ecosystem states under climate change and clarify the relative importance of local wind vs. remote oceanic processes. The results will advance the synthesis of Arrigo's prior observational studies on under-ice blooms and carbon export into a predictive modeling context.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have modeled polynyas or blooms in isolation, this proposal uniquely couples high-resolution physics with a biogeochemical model explicitly configured to test the interactive effects of wind, meltwater, and circulation on interannual bloom variability, a synthesis not yet achieved in pre-2026 literature for these key regions.

proposals.title: Submesoscale Eddy-Driven Phytoplankton Patchiness and Biogeochemical Fluxes in Arctic Polynyas: A High-Resolution Sensor-Based Study
proposals.motivation: Polynyas are critical hotspots for primary production and carbon export in the Arctic, but their internal dynamics are poorly resolved. Pre-2026 satellite analyses, including Arrigo's work on under-ice blooms, show high spatial variability, suggesting that mesoscale and submesoscale processes within polynyas, such as eddies, may control bloom initiation, species composition, and vertical export. Understanding these fine-scale physical-biological couplings is essential for accurately predicting how Arctic marine ecosystems will respond to ongoing sea ice loss and changes in stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cyclonic and anticyclonic eddies generated within or advected into polynyas create distinct patches of phytoplankton biomass and community structure by modulating light availability, nutrient supply (particularly iron and nitrate), and mixed-layer depth at sub-polynya scales.
proposals.method: We will deploy a suite of autonomous platforms (gliders and profiling floats equipped with high-resolution bio-optical and environmental sensors) during the spring bloom in a key polynya (e.g., the Northeast Water Polynya). Sensors will include fast-repetition-rate fluorometers, hyperspectral radiometers, and nitrate sensors. Glider transects will map eddy fields identified via satellite altimetry. We will apply wavelet analysis to quantify patch scales and use a diagnostic model linking eddy vorticity to vertical velocity and inferred nutrient fluxes to explain observed phytoplankton patchiness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first in-situ, high-resolution quantification of how sub-polynya eddies structure phytoplankton communities and influence the efficiency of the biological carbon pump in the Arctic. It will directly inform the parameterization of subgrid-scale processes in regional biogeochemical models, improving forecasts of Arctic primary production and carbon sequestration under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed polynya-scale blooms, this study uniquely targets the sub-polynya, eddy-resolving scale using integrated autonomous sensor suites, directly testing the mechanistic link between eddy dynamics and phytoplankton patchiness—a scale gap in pre-2026 Arctic oceanography.

proposals.title: Marine Mammal-Mediated Fertilization: Quantifying the Influence of Seal Aggregations on Under-Ice Primary Production in the Seasonal Ice Zone
proposals.motivation: Traditional models of polar marine ecosystems treat phytoplankton blooms as bottom-up processes driven by light and nutrient availability, with top predators as passive beneficiaries. However, the role of apex consumers in actively enhancing primary productivity via nutrient recycling (the 'whale pump' concept) is recognized but poorly quantified, especially for seals in the high Arctic. As climate change rapidly alters ice cover and ecosystem structure, understanding these biotic feedbacks is critical for predicting future carbon cycling and food web dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that localized aggregations of ice-associated seals (e.g., ringed seals, bearded seals) create biogeochemical hotspots by releasing nitrogen- and iron-rich excreta, thereby stimulating under-ice phytoplankton biomass and influencing bloom phenology in the marginal ice zone.
proposals.method: We will integrate satellite-derived sea ice concentration and chlorophyll-a data (MODIS/VIIRS) with historical seal haul-out and foraging location data from telemetry studies (e.g., SAT tags). Targeted field campaigns in the Chukchi and Beaufort Seas will measure vertical nutrient profiles, particulate organic carbon, and nitrogen isotopes (δ15N) in phytoplankton near seal aggregations versus control sites. A coupled 1-D physical-biological model (configured with pre-2026 frameworks like the Regional Ocean Modeling System with biogeochemistry) will be used to isolate the nutrient flux contribution from seal activity from physical processes like upwelling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative estimate of seal-mediated nutrient fertilization in the Arctic, directly testing a top-down control on primary production. It will advance our mechanistic understanding of polar carbon export by linking marine mammal behavior to phytoplankton bloom dynamics, offering a more integrated framework for ecosystem models under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While the 'whale pump' is documented, the concept is largely unexplored for seals and specifically for under-ice blooms. This inverts the classic causal direction by treating a high-trophic-level consumer as a potential driver, rather than a result, of localized high primary production.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Seal-Mediated Nutrient Cycling in Enhancing Primary Production in Antarctic Coastal Polynyas
proposals.motivation: Antarctic coastal polynyas are critical sites of intense phytoplankton blooms and carbon export, but nutrient supply mechanisms, particularly iron, remain incompletely quantified. Pre-2026 research has established the importance of marine mammals in biogeochemical cycling, yet the specific impact of seals as vectors for nutrient recycling within polynya food webs is poorly understood. This study addresses a key gap in linking upper-trophic-level ecology with the physical and biogeochemical drivers of high-latitude primary production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the foraging and excretion behaviors of Antarctic seals, particularly Weddell and crabeater seals, actively recycle limiting nutrients (iron and nitrogen) within polynyas, creating localized nutrient hotspots that enhance the magnitude and spatial patterning of phytoplankton blooms.
proposals.method: We will integrate field observations with a 3D coupled physical-biogeochemical model (ROMS-BEC). Fieldwork in the Ross Sea Polynya will measure seal population density, foraging dives (via telemetry), and collect water column profiles for nutrients, chlorophyll, and particulate iron near seal haul-outs. These data will parameterize a seal nutrient module that simulates vertical and horizontal nutrient transport via excretion. Model scenarios will compare primary production with and without seal-mediated cycling, using Lagrangian particle tracking to assess bloom development.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative estimate of seal-driven nutrient fertilization in an Antarctic polynya, directly linking marine mammal behavior to phytoplankton dynamics. It will advance our understanding of biological feedbacks within polar marine ecosystems and improve predictions of how changing seal populations, due to climate or harvesting pressures, might influence carbon fixation in these globally important regions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Arrigo and others has focused on physical drivers of polynya productivity, this study innovatively inverts the traditional view to test how apex consumers can act as bottom-up drivers. It uniquely integrates marine mammal behavioral ecology into a high-resolution biogeochemical model of a polar shelf sea.

ideas.title: Inverting Ice-Production-Climate Feedbacks: Using Sedimentary Production Records to Constrain Phytoplankton-Driven Mixed Layer Modulation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using high-resolution sedimentary records of primary production and phytoplankton community structure to inversely model not just past sea ice conditions, but also the potential for biologically mediated changes in mixed layer depth (MLD). The core hypothesis is that specific production regimes, indicated by the sediment proxies, could have altered water column stability through mechanisms like light absorption or carbon export, creating a feedback that either reinforced or diminished ice cover. This approach transforms a simple inverse problem (ice from production) into a dynamic system inversion, where the sedimentary archive is used to diagnose the strength and direction of a coupled bio-physical climate feedback across key climatic transitions.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Test for Wind- and Freshening-Driven Destratification in Subpolar Gyres and Its Trophic Consequences
ideas.description: This research tests a specific hypothesis: that amplified precipitation and runoff at high latitudes, combined with strong wind forcing, can lead to a net decrease in seasonal stratification in subpolar regions, counter to the global trend. The approach integrates satellite and in-situ data to detect a coherent freshening and mixing signal, uses high-resolution regional climate models to attribute changes to the proposed mechanism, and employs plankton time-series and trait-based models to quantify the consequent shift from a stratified, picoplankton-dominated system to a more mixed, diatom-supporting one. This mechanistic framework transforms a vague challenge into a falsifiable prediction with defined metrics for success.

Concurrent water column profiling with genomic and visual plankton surveys will characterize the consumer communities responsible for remineralization. The integrated dataset will explicitly test how food web structure modulates the coupling between increased production and enhanced benthic carbon supply.

ideas.title: Biological Modification of Ocean Stratification via Phytoplankton-Mediated Turbulence
ideas.description: This research inverts the classical causality in oceanography where mixed layer depth (MLD) is a physical driver of phytoplankton growth. Drawing an analogy from geomorphology, where vegetation growth directly alters river flow and channel structure, we propose that phytoplankton communities can actively modify their physical environment. The analogous solution treats biological activity as a direct forcing in physical models. Adapted to oceanography, the idea is to develop a coupled biophysical model where phytoplankton effects—such as altered light attenuation profiles (impacting solar heating) and the generation of turbulence via sinking aggregates or swimming—are explicitly included as terms that can modify turbulent kinetic energy and stratification. This would test the hypothesis that phytoplankton growth can create a feedback loop capable of significantly altering mixed layer depth, thereby challenging the assumption that MLD is solely a top-down physical constraint.

ideas.title: Assess changes in pelagic-benthic coupling strength using a metabolic scaling framework
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from terrestrial ecosystem ecology, where metabolic scaling and stoichiometric frameworks are used to quantify how primary production drives carbon transfer to soils. The analogous problem—understanding how increased plant productivity alters litterfall quality and soil coupling—is solved by measuring fluxes, pool sizes, and elemental ratios to model coupling strength. Adapted to the marine context, the idea is to investigate how increased oceanic primary production modifies the carbon export flux and the degree of pelagic-benthic coupling by applying a similar framework. Specifically, it will treat the water column as a processing system, analyzing how changes in production alter the stoichiometric quality and lability of sinking particles. This approach will model coupling strength as a scalable function of surface production rate and particle composition, enabling predictions of future changes in benthic community metabolism and seafloor carbon sequestration under scenarios of increased ocean productivity.

ideas.title: A Data-Assimilating Biogeochemical Model Informed by BGC-Argo Floats for Satellite Primary Production Product Validation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses autonomous biogeochemical Argo float profiles as the primary data source to constrain and update a three-dimensional, coupled physical-biogeochemical ocean model via data assimilation. The model, run at eddy-permitting resolution, generates daily, gap-free fields of net primary production and related biogeochemical variables. These high-resolution model fields, validated internally against the assimilated float data, are then spatially and temporally aggregated to directly match the footprint and overpass timing of satellite sensors, enabling a statistically robust, scale-aware validation of the satellite-derived primary production estimates across entire ocean basins.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Ice History from Production Trends via Seismic Deconvolution Analogy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse solution from reflection seismology to paleoceanography. In seismology, deconvolution recovers subsurface structure from a convolved seismic trace. Here, the reconstructed primary production record from a sediment core is analogized as a convolved signal. It results from the 'convolution' of a potential production driver (e.g., solar irradiance, nutrient availability) with a time-varying 'filter' representing ice-cover attenuation. The solution involves formulating the forward model: Production(t) = Driver(t) * (1 - IceEffect(t)), where IceEffect is a function of ice thickness/duration. The inverse problem is then solved using optimization or linear inverse techniques (e.g., Tikhonov regularization) to find the ice-cover history that, when applied to an estimated driver, best fits the observed production proxy data. This provides a quantitative, model-based inference of past ice variability directly from biological productivity trends.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Inversion Techniques for Subsurface Phytoplankton Profiling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from seismic exploration in geophysics to the problem of estimating subsurface phytoplankton biomass. In the analogous field, seismic inversion uses surface measurements of reflected sound waves and physics-based models to iteratively estimate the 3D distribution of subsurface rock properties. The adapted research idea applies this inversion framework to ocean color remote sensing. It treats the ocean as an optical medium, using measured water-leaving radiance (the surface signal) and a bio-optical model that describes how light interacts with water and particles. An iterative optimization algorithm would then be employed to invert this model, solving for the vertical profile of phytoplankton biomass that best matches the observed ocean color spectrum, thereby providing a depth-resolved estimate beyond the surface layer.

ideas.title: Dynamic Ecological Network Resilience to Non-Monotonic Stratification Changes
ideas.description: This research integrates ecological network analysis with investigations into regionally variable ocean stratification trends. It models the diatom-Phaeocystis competitive network not under a simple, globally increasing stratification scenario, but under projected regional decreases (e.g., from increased storm frequency or meltwater inputs) and increased spatial heterogeneity. The novel synergy is assessing how the stability, modularity, and keystone roles within the phytoplankton interaction network respond to these complex physical drivers, predicting where and why community shifts may defy broad-scale expectations.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Infer past stratification from sediments using a paleo-assimilation framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the data assimilation and reanalysis techniques from numerical weather prediction to solve the inverse problem in paleoceanography. The analogous field's solution involves using sparse observational data to constrain a dynamic model's past states via statistical inversion. Here, an ensemble of 1D ocean biogeochemical models, simulating phytoplankton community dynamics (diatoms vs. Phaeocystis) in response to mixed layer depth (MLD) and nutrient availability, will be used. The sedimentary fossil assemblages (the 'paleo-observations') are assimilated into this model framework. By running the assimilation inversely, the method statistically constrains the ensemble to reconstruct a physically and ecologically consistent, high-resolution time series of historical MLD and carbon export, moving beyond simple transfer functions to a dynamic, process-based reconstruction.

ideas.title: Applying Atmospheric Boundary Layer Dynamics to Challenge Ocean Stratification Assumptions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that climate change will uniformly increase ocean stratification by drawing an analogy from atmospheric science. In meteorology, the initial assumption of uniformly increasing atmospheric stability under global warming was overturned by studying regional exceptions where surface heating, wind shear, or convection decreased local stratification. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate whether analogous regional oceanographic forcing anomalies—such as altered wind mixing, freshwater input, or lateral advection—could cause decreases in water column stratification in specific areas. By applying high-resolution regional modeling and targeted observational analysis similar to atmospheric boundary layer studies, this project aims to identify and understand ocean basins or coastal regions where dynamic processes might counteract the general thermal stratification trend, thereby altering predicted community shifts in unexpected ways.

ideas.title: Adapting Microbial Source Tracking to Link Phytoplankton Shifts to Production Increases
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from microbial source tracking (MST) in environmental microbiology to the study of phytoplankton. In MST, host-specific genetic markers are used to attribute fecal pollution in water to specific animal sources by detecting source-associated microbial community signatures. Here, we adapt this by developing a panel of taxon-specific genetic markers for key phytoplankton functional groups (e.g., diazotrophs, diatoms, dinoflagellates). Applying quantitative eDNA metabarcoding with these markers to time-series samples allows us to track shifts in these functional communities. By correlating these shifts with concurrent, independent measurements of primary production (e.g., from satellite chlorophyll-a or in situ carbon uptake), we can move beyond general community description to identify which specific phytoplankton subgroups are functionally responsible for observed production increases.

ideas.title: A Standardized Observational Network to Test Biogeographic Patterns in Southern Ocean Phytoplankton
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from epidemiology's use of multi-site cohort studies to generalize local health findings, this idea adapts the solution of standardized, coordinated data collection. The analogous problem—determining if a local association is universal—is solved by implementing identical protocols across diverse locations to ensure comparability. Adapted to the Southern Ocean context, the research idea is to establish a standardized observational network across its major sectors. Using identical sensor suites and harmonized biological sampling protocols on multiple platforms, this network would generate a directly comparable dataset to conclusively test whether the link between mixed layer depth and phytoplankton community structure observed in the Ross Sea is a regional feature or a circumpolar principle governing Southern Ocean productivity.

ideas.title: Genetically-informed stoichiometric trait mapping for dynamic Earth system model parameterization
ideas.description: This research integrates genetic metabarcoding data on phytoplankton community composition directly into the functional trait modules of global climate models. By linking detected taxa to their specific elemental (C:N:P) and macromolecular stoichiometry, the project generates spatially and temporally resolved maps of community-aggregated traits that govern carbon export and nutrient cycling. These dynamic, observationally constrained trait fields are then used to parameterize and validate the plankton functional type schemes within ESMs. This approach enables models to project carbon-climate feedbacks with greater fidelity by mechanistically representing how genetic shifts in phytoplankton communities under warming and nutrient changes will alter global biogeochemical cycles.

ideas.title: Mechanistic classification and nested modeling of shelf denitrification hot spots
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a single-region downscaling by first creating a high-resolution mechanistic map of the target shelf (e.g., Laptev Sea) classifying it into Benthic-Pelagic Functional Units (BPFUs) based on sediment type, bottom current regimes, and historical organic carbon deposition. A nested modeling system then couples a regional physical-biogeochemical model, providing carbon export fluxes, to unit-specific 1D reaction-transport sub-models for each major BPFU class. These sub-models are parameterized and validated using targeted field data (sediment cores, porewater profiles, incubation experiments) strategically collected within each BPFU. This approach quantifies denitrification hot spots by directly linking them to identifiable and mappable shelf features, producing a transferable framework for other Arctic shelves.

ideas.title: Adapting Atmospheric Deposition Models to Quantify Marine Carbon Export
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from atmospheric science to the problem of quantifying marine carbon export. In atmospheric science, inferential models combine point measurements of aerosol concentrations with meteorological data and empirically derived deposition velocities to calculate the flux of material to the surface (dry/wet deposition). We adapt this framework to the ocean. The adapted research idea involves developing a model that uses upper ocean POC concentration (the analog to atmospheric aerosol concentration) and a parameterized 'sedimentation velocity' (the analog to deposition velocity), which is a function of particle properties (size, density, stickiness) and local hydrodynamics (mixed layer depth, turbulence). This model would generate a continuous field of carbon export flux, transforming sparse, point-in-time sediment trap data into a scalable tool for quantifying new carbon export from increased primary production across entire ocean regions.

ideas.title: Mechanistic, regionally-calibrated model for carbon export from new production
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-method observational campaign to directly measure particle export flux and sinking particle spectra in the target region. These data are used to develop and calibrate a process-based particle flux model that incorporates region-specific zooplankton community composition, aggregate formation dynamics, and ballast mineral effects. The calibrated model is then driven by observations of new production to generate robust, locally-validated estimates of carbon export. This approach replaces flawed analogies with a mechanistic framework grounded in direct regional measurements.

ideas.title: Applying Portfolio Theory to Predict Carbon Export from Phytoplankton Community Structure
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from financial portfolio theory, where overall portfolio performance is modeled from constituent assets and their correlations, to marine biogeochemistry. The analogous problem of predicting a system's output (financial return / carbon export) from its components (assets / phytoplankton groups) is solved by moving beyond simple additive models to include interaction terms. The adapted research idea proposes treating the phytoplankton community as a 'biological portfolio' and developing a model where carbon export flux is a function of the weighted export traits of key phytoplankton types plus the covariance terms between them, which capture ecological interactions. This model would be calibrated using coupled phytoplankton group measurements and sediment trap data, providing a more mechanistic and predictive link between community shifts and carbon export efficiency.

ideas.title: Quantitative eDNA framework: Integrating ddPCR, pigment analysis, and metabarcoding to calibrate phytoplankton shifts to production.
ideas.description: This redesigned system directly overcomes the fatal flaw of relative abundance data by integrating three complementary methods. Environmental DNA metabarcoding is used to track fine-scale community composition shifts across a wide taxonomic range. Concurrent quantitative ddPCR assays for key functional groups (e.g., diatoms, cyanobacteria) provide absolute gene copy numbers per volume of water, anchoring the metabarcoding data to an absolute scale. Finally, traditional microscopy and pigment analysis (Chl-a, diagnostic pigments via HPLC) are performed on the same samples to calibrate DNA signals to cell counts, biovolume, and biomass, enabling a robust, quantitative link between genetic signals and actual production increases.

ideas.title: Hybrid Physical-Digital Ecotron for Emergent Phytoplankton Dynamics
ideas.description: This approach couples a precisely controlled laboratory mesocosm with a real-time, trait-based ecological simulation to overcome the oversimplification of traditional tank experiments. Physical parameters (stratification, mixing intensity, light, nutrients) are manipulated in the tank and continuously monitored, feeding data into a digital model containing a vast, realistic phytoplankton trait space. The simulated community competition, driven by the physical conditions, predicts shifts in bulk properties (e.g., biomass, size distribution) which are then used to adaptively adjust the physical regime in the tank, closing the feedback loop. This creates a testbed where macro-scale emergent outcomes from micro-scale trait interactions can be studied under controlled but ecologically complex scenarios.

ideas.title: Adapting Airborne LIDAR Terrain Classification for Phytoplankton Community Profiling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from remote sensing, where airborne LIDAR data is processed with machine learning to classify terrain types rapidly over vast scales, to the problem of rapid phytoplankton community analysis. In the analogous field, raw LIDAR point clouds are classified into categories like vegetation and urban structures based on spatial features. The adapted research idea treats multi-parameter flow cytometric data (forward scatter, side scatter, and multiple fluorescence wavelengths) as a high-dimensional 'optical point cloud' representing individual phytoplankton cells. By applying and refining similar automated classification algorithms, this method would enable the rapid, in-situ segmentation and quantification of phytoplankton groups (e.g., cyanobacteria, diatoms, coccolithophores) based on their intrinsic optical properties, drastically speeding up community analysis compared to manual microscopy or slower genetic techniques.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Phytoplankton Cytometer with Integrated Optical and Genetic Probing
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the low taxonomic resolution of pure optical flow cytometry by combining it with genetic identification in a single automated platform. A microfluidic device first performs initial size and pigment-based sorting of particles from a continuous seawater intake. Selected cells are then encapsulated into droplets where they undergo rapid, automated fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) with taxon-specific nucleic acid probes. The resulting droplets are analyzed by a final detection module that reads both the original optical signals and the new genetic probe fluorescence, enabling accurate classification of phytoplankton down to the genus or species level at near-real-time speeds.

ideas.title: Dynamic identification of nutrient constraint emergence under progressive light-use efficiency gains
ideas.description: This research moves beyond testing a static assumption to dynamically model how the primary limiting factor shifts under intensification. Using a process-based crop model coupled with a nutrient economy module, it simulates future production pathways where light-use efficiency is progressively enhanced through genetic, management, and climate interventions. The analysis identifies regional and temporal tipping points where nutrient supply, governed by soil biogeochemistry and fertilizer logistics, fails to meet the demand driven by higher photosynthetic potential, thereby mapping the future landscapes of limitation.

ideas.title: Calibrating Algorithmic Stability: Using Paleo-Ecosystem Reconstructions to Test Primary Production Models Under Regime Shifts
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the inverse paleo-reconstruction of mixed layer depth and carbon export with the challenge of algorithmic parameter stability. It uses sedimentary diatom/Phaeocystis fossils to identify past periods of major ecosystem shift (e.g., dominance changes, altered stratification). For each reconstructed paleo-regime, it applies modern primary production algorithms, testing whether a single, stable parameter set can accurately back-calculate the independently reconstructed carbon export. This directly tests algorithmic robustness against real, non-stationary ecosystem history, moving beyond theoretical challenge to empirical validation using Earth's paleo-record.

ideas.title: Microfluidic Lab-on-a-Chip for Simulating Oceanic Stratification Gradients
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from biomedical microfluidics, where lab-on-a-chip devices create stable chemical gradients to study cell behavior, this idea adapts that solution to physical oceanography and ecology. The analogous solution involves using engineered micro-channels and laminar flow to establish precise, controllable gradients. Adapted to the original research seed, this means designing a microfluidic mesocosm—a 'lab-on-a-chip' for oceans—that uses layered flows to replicate stratification and mixing regimes at a miniature scale. This system isolates the effect of these physical drivers on phytoplankton competition by allowing for the manipulation and real-time, microscopic observation of population dynamics at sharp pycnoclines and gradient interfaces that are difficult to study in large tanks or the open sea.

ideas.title: Apply Economic Market Share Dynamics to Model Phytoplankton Competitive Exclusion
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from economics/industrial organization, where the Lotka-Volterra framework is used to model market share competition between firms. The analogous solution treats firms as species competing for market resources, modeling their interactions as a dynamic network to predict outcomes like dominance or coexistence. Adapted to the ecological context, we model competitive exclusion between diatoms and Phaeocystis by treating them as competitors for the 'market share' of essential resources like silicate and iron. Their interactions are framed as an ecological network where nodes are functional traits or resource pools, and edges are competitive strengths derived from growth rates, grazing pressure, and environmental tolerances. This approach will quantify the conditions leading to a regime shift, predicting when one phytoplankton group will exclude the other based on simulated resource competition dynamics.

ideas.title: Parameterizing Stoichiometry via a Community Trait Distribution Function
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from stellar astrophysics, where the Initial Mass Function (IMF) provides a statistical parameterization of stellar populations for galaxy-scale models. The analogous problem is scaling from individual organism stoichiometry to ecosystem-level biogeochemistry in Earth system models. The adapted solution is to develop a Community Stoichiometry Distribution Function (CSDF)—a statistical framework that maps environmental conditions (temperature, nutrient availability) to the probability distribution of key stoichiometric traits (e.g., C:N:P) within a model grid cell's biological community. This CSDF would be embedded as a dynamic module within global climate models, allowing carbon and nutrient cycle projections to respond to changing community composition and trait distributions, thereby improving the mechanistic realism and accuracy of carbon cycle projections without the computational cost of tracking individual species.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Inversion Techniques to Infer Mixed Layer Depth from Satellite Altimetry
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from geophysical seismology to the oceanographic problem of estimating mixed layer depth (MLD) synoptically. In seismology, subsurface structure is inferred from surface wave data using inversion techniques. The analogous solution involves using a forward model of seismic wave propagation and an inverse method to estimate layer depths. Adapted to this research context, satellite altimetry measurements—specifically sea surface height (SSH) anomalies, surface roughness, and their gradients—are treated as the observable surface signal. A physical forward model, incorporating known relationships between MLD, ocean stratification, and surface expressions in SSH, is developed. An inverse method (e.g., variational assimilation, machine learning inversion) is then applied to these synoptic satellite data across the Southern Ocean to iteratively solve for the MLD field that best explains the observed surface signatures, providing a novel remote-sensing-based estimate of this critical oceanographic parameter.

ideas.title: Adaptive Parameterization via Regime Detection and Bayesian Updating
ideas.description: This idea challenges the stability of primary production algorithm parameters under fundamental ecosystem change by drawing an analogy from structural engineering. There, models for seismic response are calibrated to local soil conditions, and parameters become unstable if the building is moved to a different seismic regime. The analogous solution involves continuous monitoring for a regime shift and using Bayesian methods to update core model parameters post-shift. Adapted to ecology, the research proposes developing algorithms that integrate real-time ecological regime detection (using indicators like community composition) with a Bayesian parameter-updating framework. When a shift is detected, the algorithm's foundational constants (e.g., photosynthetic efficiency, chlorophyll-to-biomass ratios) are dynamically and probabilistically recalibrated using incoming data, ensuring the model remains stable and accurate despite the underlying ecosystem transformation.
